stem: A 34-year-old male is found to have a serum calcium level of 12.8 mg/dl (Normal: 8.5-10.25 mg/dl) with elevated PTH. Ultrasonography reveals a 1.5 x 1.5 cm mass in the right lower lobe of the thyroid gland. Which of the following is true regarding this condition?
options: A normal DEXA scan rules out the need for surgery
MIBI followed by focussed parathyroidectomy to be done
No surgery is required, only surveillance
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Hypercalcemia with elevated PTH confirms primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT).
• A 1.5 x 1.5 cm mass in the lower pole of the thyroid is most likely a parathyroid adenoma, the most common cause of PHPT.
• 99mTc-sestamibi (MIBI) scan is used along with ultrasonography to localize the hyperfunctioning gland before surgery.
• Focussed parathyroidectomy is the treatment of choice for a single adenoma and is minimally invasive.
• Surgery is indicated based on biochemical and imaging evidence, regardless of normal DEXA or creatinine clearance.
• Surveillance alone is insufficient in symptomatic or biochemically confirmed cases.


Other Options:
Option A: Normal DEXA does not rule out the need for surgery.
Option C: Surveillance alone is not appropriate with a confirmed adenoma.
Option D: Creatinine clearance >80 ml/min is not an indication for surgery.

stem: A middle-aged woman presents with flank pain and painful micturition. The pain was relieved by IV analgesics given at a nearby primary healthcare centre. The patient is diagnosed with ureteric colic due to ureteric calculi. What is the best diagnostic modality in this case?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Non-contrast CT of the kidneys, ureters, and bladder (NCCT KUB) is the gold-standard first-line imaging modality for suspected ureteric stones due to its very high sensitivity and specificity for detecting calculi.
• NCCT accurately identifies stone size, location, number, and degree of obstruction, which are critical for management decisions.
• CECT (contrast CT) is not recommended for stone diagnosis because contrast obscures calculi and adds unnecessary risk of contrast-induced nephropathy.
• USG KUB is less sensitive, especially for mid-ureteric stones, and is operator-dependent; it is useful in pregnancy or when radiation avoidance is essential.
• Ureteroscopy is a therapeutic intervention, not a primary diagnostic modality.


Other Options:
Option B: CECT is not used for diagnosing stones due to contrast interference and nephrotoxicity risk.
Option C: USG KUB has lower sensitivity and may miss stones.
Option D: Ureteroscopy is invasive and used for stone removal rather than initial diagnosis.

stem: Identify the marked structure:
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The marked structure represents Corpora amylacea, small, rounded, eosinophilic bodies composed of glycoproteins and carbohydrates.
• Commonly found in the prostate, especially in benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), within prostatic acini.
• Present in astrocytic processes of the CNS, often associated with aging or neurodegenerative diseases.
• Generally benign and age-related, but may be linked to conditions like multiple sclerosis or Alzheimer's disease. They are not calcified; their presence is distinct from structures like corpora arenacea or psammoma bodies.
• Useful as a histopathological marker in aging tissues and benign lesions.
Other Options:
Option B: Corpora arenacea are calcified structures in the pineal gland, visible on X-ray and increasing with age. Pineal gland showing corpora arenacea.


Option C: Lewy bodies are aggregates of alpha-synuclein and ubiquitin in neurons, characteristic of Parkinson's disease and Lewy body dementia.
Option D: Psammoma bodies are laminated, calcified structures seen in tumors such as papillary thyroid carcinoma, serous ovarian carcinoma, meningiomas, and mesotheliomas.

stem: A 25-year-old patient sustains a fall from the 2nd floor of a building and is brought to the emergency department. Which of the following is not an adjunct to the primary survey?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The primary survey (ABCDE) in ATLS focuses on identifying and treating immediately life-threatening conditions.
• Adjuncts to the primary survey are rapid bedside investigations that do not delay resuscitation.
• Portable chest X-ray and pelvic X-ray are routine adjuncts during the primary survey.
• Extended FAST (eFAST) is a bedside ultrasound used to detect free fluid, pneumothorax, or hemothorax during the primary survey.
• CT abdomen and pelvis requires patient stability and transfer out of the resuscitation area.
• Therefore, CT scanning is part of the secondary survey, not an adjunct to the primary survey.


Other Options:
Option A: Pelvic X-ray is a standard adjunct to detect pelvic fractures and hemorrhage.
Option B: Chest X-ray is routinely performed during the primary survey.
Option C: EFAST is a rapid bedside adjunct used during the primary survey.

stem: A 20-year-old male presents with acute epigastric pain and fever. He has a past history of cholelithiasis. On examination, abdominal guarding and rigidity present. His serum amylase, lipase are raised and the patient has leucocytosis. What is true regarding his condition?
options: Serum amylase remains elevated even after 30 days of resolution of acute pancreatitis
Serum lipase is more specific than serum amylase
Increase in the level of serum lipase is directly proportional to the severity
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The clinical features are suggestive of acute pancreatitis, commonly due to gallstones in young adults.
• Serum lipase is more specific than amylase for acute pancreatitis, as amylase can rise in salivary gland disease, bowel obstruction, or renal failure.
• Lipase remains elevated longer than amylase, increasing diagnostic reliability.
• Diagnosis requires at least two of three criteria: characteristic abdominal pain, enzyme elevation >3x ULN or imaging findings.
• Serum amylase normalizes within 3-5 days, not weeks.
• The degree of enzyme elevation does not correlate with severity, severity is assessed using clinical and radiologic scoring systems.
Ranson Prognostic Criteria for Acute Pancreatitis:
ParameterNon-gallstone pancreatitisGallstone pancreatitis
Age>55 years>70 years
Blood glucose level>200 mg/dL>220 mg/dL
White blood cell count>16,000 cells/mm3>18,000 cells/mm3
Serum LDH level>350 IU/L>400 IU/L
Serum AST level>250 IU/L>250 IU/L

Ranson's prognostic criteria after 48 hours of admission:
ParameterNon-gallstone pancreatitisGallstone pancreatitis
Hematocrit fall>10%>10%
Serum calcium level<8 mg/dL<8 mg/dL
Base deficit>4 mEq/L>5 mEq/L
BUN level increase>5 mg/dL despite IV fluid hydration>2 mg/dL despite IV fluid hydration
Fluid sequestration>6 L>4 L
PaO2<60 mm HgNot available

Ranson score >=3 defines severe pancreatitis.
Score Interpretation:
0 to 2 points: 0% to 3% mortality
3 to 4 points: 15% mortality
5 to 6 points: 40% mortality
7 to 11: nearly 100% mortality

Other Options:
Option A: Serum amylase typically normalizes within 3-5 days after acute pancreatitis.
Option C: Hypocalcemia, not hypercalcemia, is common in severe pancreatitis due to fat saponification.
Option D: Serum lipase levels do not correlate with disease severity.

stem: A 34-year-old female presents with a lump in her left breast for 2 years. On examination, a 6 x 4 cm mass is palpated. Dimpling of the skin and bloody discharge from the nipple is present. The lesion is invading the pectoralis major muscle. Ipsilateral Level I and Level II matted axillary lymph nodes present. There was no distant metastasis. What is the TNM staging (AJCC 8th)?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Tumour size is 6 cm in greatest dimension, which corresponds to T3 (>5 cm).
• Invasion of the pectoralis major muscle alone does NOT qualify as chest wall invasion in AJCC staging.
• Skin dimpling without ulceration, peau d'orange, or satellite nodules does not meet criteria for T4b.
• Ipsilateral Level I and II matted axillary lymph nodes correspond to N2 disease.
• Absence of distant metastasis classifies the tumour as M0.
• Hence, the correct TNM stage is T3N2M0.


TNM Staging of Carcinoma Breast:
Tumor (T)Description
TXPrimary tumor cannot be assessed
T0No evidence of primary tumor
Tis (DCIS)Ductal carcinoma in situ
Tis (Paget)Paget disease not associated with invasive carcinoma or DCIS
T1<= 2 cm in greatest dimension (1mi: <= 0.1 cm, 1a: 0.1 to 0.5 cm, 1b: 0.5 to 1 cm, 1c: 1 to 2 cm)
T2>2 but <=5 cm in greatest dimension
T3>5 cm in greatest dimension
T4Tumor of any size and: 4a-chest wall involvement (except pectoralis major), 4b-skin involvement in the form of ulceration, peau d'orange, satellite nodules, 4c-4a + 4b, 4d-Inflammatory breast carcinoma

Nodes (N)Description
N1 micRefers to micrometastasis to Lymph node
N1Ipsilateral mobile axillary lymph nodes
N2AIpsilateral fixed or matted axillary lymph nodes
N2BIpsilateral internal mammary without axillary lymph node involvement
N3AIpsilateral infraclavicular lymph nodes
N3BIpsilateral internal mammary and axillary lymph nodes
N3CIpsilateral supraclavicular lymph nodes

Distant metastasis (M)Description
M0No Metastasis
M1Metastasis present

StagesT, NM
Stage 0Tis N0M0
Stage IAT1 N0M0
Stage IBT0,1 N1micM0
Stage IIAT0,1 N1 (or) T2 N0M0
Stage IIBT2 N1 (or) T3 N0M0
Stage IIIAT0,1,2,3 N2 (or) T3 N1M0
Stage IIIBT4 N0,1,2M0
Stage IIICAny T, N3M0
Stage IVAny T, Any NM1

c: clinical staging (eg. cT1b)
p: pathological (histological) staging
y: stage following systemic or radiation therapy (either prior to surgery or as a primary treatment)
r: staging at the time of retreatment or recurrence for disease progression
Suffix for TNM staging: m: multiple primary tumours of the same histology in the same organ, eg. T2(m) or T2(5), the latter meaning that there are five primary tumours.

Other Options:
Option A: T4b requires skin ulceration, edema (peau d'orange), or satellite nodules, which are not described.
Option C: T4a requires chest wall invasion excluding pectoralis muscle, which is not present here.
Option D: No distant metastasis is mentioned, hence M1 staging is incorrect.

stem: An 84-year-old man with a history of stroke 2 years ago, who is on prolonged bed rest, develops a pressure ulcer with partial thickness skin loss and a clean base. What is the grade of his pressure sore?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Pressure ulcers result from prolonged pressure exceeding capillary closing pressure, leading to tissue ischemia and necrosis.
• Grade 2 pressure sores are characterized by partial-thickness skin loss involving the epidermis and dermis.
• They typically present as a shallow ulcer with a clean base or blister.
• There is no involvement of subcutaneous tissue, muscle, or bone in Grade 2 ulcers.
• Prolonged immobilization, advanced age, and neurological deficits are major risk factors.
• Early identification and pressure off-loading prevent progression to deeper ulcers.


Staging of Pressure Sores:
StageDescription
1Non-blanchable erythema without a breach in the epidermis
2Partial thickness skin loss involving the epidermis and dermis
3Full thickness skin loss extending into the subcutaneous tissue but not through underlying fascia
4Full thickness skin loss through fascia with extensive tissue destruction, maybe involving muscle, bone, tendon or joint



Other Options:
Option A: Grade 1 shows intact skin with non-blanchable erythema only.
Option B: Grade 4 involves full-thickness tissue loss with exposed muscle or bone.
Option C: Grade 3 involves full-thickness skin loss extending into subcutaneous tissue.

stem: During ward rounds, you see a patient who is admitted with a ca esophagus. What is the most likely presentation in this patient?
options: Dysphagia of solids followed by liquids
Dysphagia of liquids followed by solids
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Carcinoma esophagus produces a mechanical obstruction, leading initially to difficulty swallowing solids.
• As the tumor enlarges and the lumen narrows further, dysphagia progresses to liquids.
• This solid-to-liquid progression is characteristic of organic (structural) esophageal disease.
• Dysphagia is the most common and earliest presenting symptom of carcinoma esophagus.
• Squamous cell carcinoma commonly affects the upper and middle thirds, while adenocarcinoma affects the lower third of the esophagus.
• Associated features such as weight loss, regurgitation, and odynophagia usually appear later.


Other Options:
Option B: Dysphagia of liquids followed by solids is typical of esophageal motility disorders such as achalasia cardia.
Option C: Chest pain can occur but is not the most common presenting symptom.
Option D: Neck mass indicates advanced disease with lymph node metastasis and is not an initial presentation.

stem: What is the T stage of this oral cavity tumour?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The tumour involves the floor of the mouth and the lip, indicating invasion of adjacent structures.
• T4a (moderately advanced local disease) includes tumours that invade: 1. Cortical bone (mandible or maxilla) 2. Inferior alveolar nerve 3. Floor of the mouth 4. Skin of the face (chin or nose)
• Tumours >4 cm without adjacent structure invasion are T3, while T1-T2 are smaller lesions.
• T4b (very advanced local disease) involves invasion into the masticator space, pterygoid plates, skull base, or encasement of the internal carotid artery.
• Incisional biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis, and MRI is preferred for assessing tumour extent.
• Management typically involves surgical excision with or without adjuvant radiotherapy and chemotherapy depending on stage.


Primary Tumour (T):
CategoryDescription
TXCannot be assessed
TisCarcinoma in situ
T1Tumor <= 2 cm, depth of invasion (DOI) <= 5 mm (DOI is depth of invasion and not tumor thickness)
T2Tumor <= 2 cm, DOI >5 mm and <=10 mm or tumor > 2 cm but <=4 cm, and <=10 mm DOI
T3Tumor > 4 cm or any tumor >10 mm DOI
T4Moderately advanced or very advanced local disease
T4aModerately advanced local disease: Tumor >4 cms with DOI > 10 mm or tumor invades adjacent structures only (through cortical bone of the mandible or maxilla or involves the maxillary sinus or skin of the face). Note that superficial erosion of bone/tooth socket (alone) by a gingival primary is not sufficient to classify a tumor as T4
T4bVery advanced local disease: tumor invades the masticator space, pterygoid plates, or skull base and/or encases the internal carotid artery

Regional lymph nodes (N) - Clinical:
CategoryDescription
NXCannot be assessed
N0No regional lymph node metastasis
N1Single ipsilateral, <=3 cm and ENE (extranodal extension)-negative
N2Single ipsilateral lymph node larger than 3 cms but not greater than 6 cms, ENE-negative; Multiple ipsilateral lymph nodes, <=6 cm and ENE-negative; Bilateral or contralateral lymph nodes, <=6 cm and ENE-negative
N2aSingle ipsilateral lymph node larger than 3 cms but not greater than 6 cms, ENE-negative
N2bMultiple ipsilateral lymph nodes, <=6 cm and ENE-negative
N2cBilateral or contralateral lymph nodes, <=6 cm and ENE-negative
N3Metastasis in a lymph node larger than 6 cms and ENE-negative or metastasis in any lymph node and clinically overt ENE-positive
N3aMetastasis in a lymph node > 6 cms and ENE-negative
N3bMetastasis in any lymph node(s) and clinically overt ENE-positive

Other Options:
Option A: T2 corresponds to tumour >2 cm but <=4 cm without invasion of adjacent structures.
Option B: T4b involves very advanced local invasion into masticator space, skull base, or encasement of major vessels.
Option C: T3 is for tumours >4 cm or minor invasion into structures such as deep tongue muscles.

stem: A 30-year-old patient with peptic ulcer disease presents with abdominal pain that is not responding despite treatment in multiple hospitals. He was found to have ulcers in the jejunum and ileum. Gastric pH <2, and gastrin levels after adequate cessation of PPIs are 10 times the normal value. Identify the lesion present and the boundaries of the triangle containing it:
options: Gastrinoma; triangle formed by common bile duct, junction of D2-D3, neck and body of pancreas
Insulinoma; triangle formed by cystic duct, junction of D2-D3, neck and body of pancreas
Gastrinoma; triangle formed by cystic duct, junction of D2-D3, neck and body of pancreas
Insulinoma; triangle formed by common bile duct, junction of D2-D3, neck and body of pancreas
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The patient's features - refractory abdominal pain, multiple jejunal and ileal ulcers, gastric pH <2, and markedly elevated gastrin - indicate gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome).
• Gastrinoma triangle (Passaro's triangle) is formed by: 1. Superiorly: cystic duct 2. Inferiorly: junction of second and third parts of the duodenum (D2-D3) 3. Medially: neck and body of the pancreas
• Gastrinomas are neuroendocrine tumors that secrete excess gastrin, causing severe gastric acid hypersecretion and peptic ulcers in typical (proximal duodenum) and atypical (distal duodenum, jejunum) sites.
• They may occur sporadically or as part of MEN-1 syndrome.
• Diagnosis: 1. Serum gastrin >1000 pg/mL with gastric pH <2 2. Secretin stimulation test if gastrin 100-1000 pg/mL
• Localization for surgery: initial CT or MRI, followed by somatostatin receptor scintigraphy or endoscopic ultrasound if needed.
• Management involves surgical excision for localized gastrinoma, along with acid suppression therapy.


Other Options:
Option A: The triangle is defined by the cystic duct, not the common bile duct.
Option B & D: Insulinoma is a different neuroendocrine tumor; it does not present with hypergastrinemia or multiple distal ulcers.

stem: An intern is assisting with laparoscopic surgery, and the resident is orienting the intern to the instruments and the operating ports. Your surgeon asks you what the angle between the camera port and the working port is?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The Azimuth's angle is the angle formed between the camera port and the working port in laparoscopic surgery.
• It should be a minimum of 30° to prevent instrument clashing and ensure smooth maneuverability.
• Proper port placement is crucial for ergonomics and surgical efficiency.
• Correct azimuth angle minimizes interference between instruments and improves surgical access.
• This principle is applied in laparoscopic cholecystectomy, appendectomy, and other abdominal laparoscopic procedures.


Other Options:
Option B: Manipulation angle is the angle between two working ports; minimum 60° to avoid collisions.
Option C: Crossing angle refers to intersection of instruments inside the abdominal cavity.
Option D: Elevation angle is the angle between the working port and the horizontal plane; minimum 60° to prevent instrument collision with the abdominal wall.

stem: A 35-year-old male underwent surgery for an ileal perforation. Due to heavy contamination, only the sheath was closed, and the wound was left open. Daily wound washing was performed, and the wound was closed on postoperative day 5. By which mechanism does wound healing occur in this case?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The wound is cleaned and left open for a few days before surgical closure, which defines tertiary (delayed primary) intention.
• Tertiary intention combines primary and secondary healing: initial open period allows drainage and prevents infection, followed by surgical closure.
• Primary intention occurs when clean wounds with minimal tissue loss are closed immediately with approximation of edges.
• Secondary intention occurs when wounds are left open to heal by granulation tissue formation and contraction, usually due to significant tissue loss or devitalized edges.
• Tertiary closure is typically applied to grossly contaminated wounds without significant tissue loss, with daily irrigation and debridement prior to closure.
• This approach reduces the risk of infection while enabling eventual surgical closure.


Other Options:
Option A: Primary closure is immediate closure of clean wounds.
Option B: Secondary intention is spontaneous healing of open wounds without surgical closure.
Option D: Quaternary intention is not recognized in standard wound healing classifications.

stem: A 40-year-old patient who is a known case of Hodgkin's lymphoma with a previous history of irradiation presents with right-sided thyroid swelling. FNAC reveals papillary carcinoma of the thyroid and there is no lymph node involvement. What is the next step in management?
options: Hemithyroidectomy with modified radical neck dissection
Total thyroidectomy with modified radical neck dissection
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Papillary carcinoma of the thyroid is a differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC).
• Radiation-induced DTC is an absolute indication for total thyroidectomy, irrespective of tumor size.
• Total thyroidectomy allows better disease control, radioactive iodine ablation, and long-term surveillance using thyroglobulin.
• Hemithyroidectomy is reserved for low-risk DTC (1-4 cm) without radiation exposure, extrathyroidal extension, or multifocality.
• Prophylactic neck dissection is not indicated in the absence of clinically or radiologically involved lymph nodes.


Primary Tumor (T)Regional Lymph Nodes (N) / Distant Metastasis (M)
TX Primary cannot be assessedNX Nodes cannot be assessed
T0 No primary tumorN0 Negative nodes
T1a <=1 cm, intrathyroidalN1a Positive in levels VI & VII
T1b 1-2 cm, intrathyroidalN1b Positive unilateral, bilateral, contralateral lateral neck lymph nodes
T2 2-4 cm, intrathyroidal
T3 >4 cm limited to the thyroid or any size with minimal extrathyroidal extension (muscle, soft tissue)
T3a-4 cm limited to the thyroid
T3b-gross extrathyroidal extension involving only the strap muscle
T4a Invades subcutaneous tissue, larynx, trachea, esophagus, or RLNM0 No distant metastasis
T4b Invades prevertebral fascia or encases carotid or mediastinal vesselsM1 Distant metastasis

Other Options:
Option A: Hemithyroidectomy is inadequate in radiation-induced thyroid carcinoma.
Option B: Modified radical neck dissection is not indicated without lymph node involvement.
Option D: Neck dissection is performed only when there is proven nodal disease.

stem: One of your friends is teaching you about enhanced recovery after surgery. Which of the following he states is incorrect?
options: Epidural anaesthesia is recommended in laparoscopic surgery
Reduction of the hospital stay by 2-3 weeks
Beta-blockers are given after major surgery done under general anaesthesia to improve outcome
subject: Surgery, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols aim to reduce surgical stress, hasten recovery, and shorten hospital stay.
• Prolonged fasting is avoided; patients are encouraged minimal perioperative starvation with early oral intake.
• ERAS promotes minimally invasive procedures, effective multimodal analgesia, and early mobilization.
• Early removal of nasogastric tubes, avoidance of excessive IV fluids, and structured discharge planning are core principles.
• These measures collectively reduce complications and improve postoperative outcomes.


Other Options:
Option B: Epidural or regional Anaesthesia is recommended as part of multimodal analgesia, even in laparoscopic surgery.
Option C: ERAS significantly reduces hospital stay, often by days to weeks, depending on the procedure.
Option D: Perioperative beta-blockers are used in selected patients to reduce cardiac complications after major surgery.

stem: A chronic smoker comes with complaints of pain in his legs after walking 100 meters. On examination, he has an absent pulse in the bilateral dorsalis pedis, anterior tibial, and posterior tibial arteries, but no gangrene is present. Intermittent claudication is implicated. Which of the following statements is true regarding this condition?
options: Pain is more at night and sleep
Development of pain on the 1st step of walking
Pain aggravated by standing and relieved by bending forward
Pain develops after walking some distance and is relieved by rest
subject: Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Intermittent claudication is a manifestation of peripheral arterial disease (PAD) due to reduced arterial blood flow during exertion.
• Pain develops after walking a predictable distance and is relieved by rest, usually within a few minutes, in both sitting and standing positions.
• It represents mild to moderate chronic arterial occlusive disease with anaerobic metabolism in exercising muscles.
• The distance walked before pain onset is termed the claudication distance and is useful for disease assessment and follow-up.
• The superficial femoral artery is most commonly involved, producing calf pain; aorto-iliac disease produces Leriche's syndrome.


Other Options:
Option A: Night pain is rest pain, seen in severe PAD and worsens on lying down due to loss of gravity-assisted perfusion.
Option B: Pain on the first step is typical of osteoarthritis or plantar fasciitis.
Option C: Pain relieved by bending forward suggests neurogenic claudication, seen in lumbar spinal stenosis.

stem: A 25-year-old male with a history of a road traffic accident is brought to the emergency department. His GCS is 8/15, oxygen saturation is 85%, BP is 100/60 mmHg, and heart rate is 60 beats per minute. On auscultation his right side breath sounds were absent. What is the next step in management?
options: Secure airway, give oxygen, insert ICD, secure IV line
Administer IV dextrose followed by NCCT head
Start IV fluids, give atropine and secure airway
Evaluate for head injury and do CT-head
subject: Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The clinical features (RTA, GCS 8/15, hypoxia, absent unilateral breath sounds) are highly suggestive of tension pneumothorax, a life-threatening condition.
• According to ATLS principles, management follows ABCDE, treating what kills first.
• Airway protection is mandatory as GCS <=8 indicates the need for a definitive airway.
• Breathing must be immediately addressed with oxygen supplementation and chest decompression (needle decompression followed by ICD).
• Circulation is supported by securing IV access after airway and breathing are stabilized.
• Imaging and neurological evaluation are deferred until the patient is stabilized (secondary survey).


Other Options:
Option B: NCCT head is part of the secondary survey and should not delay airway and breathing management.
Option C: IV fluids and atropine do not address the immediate life-threatening cause of hypoxia.
Option D: CT head evaluation is inappropriate before stabilizing the airway and breathing.

stem: An elderly male patient comes with foul-smelling breath and dysphagia. A barium swallow X-ray is attached. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Surgery, Radiology, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• The combination of foul-smelling breath, dysphagia, and regurgitation suggests a Zenker's diverticulum.
• Barium swallow shows a posterior pharyngeal pouch at the pharyngoesophageal junction (C5-C6), best visualized on lateral views.
• The diverticulum results from herniation of mucosa and submucosa through Killian's triangle, a weak area between the thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus muscles.
• Symptoms may include halitosis, regurgitation of undigested food, gurgling sounds on swallowing, and aspiration.
• Zenker's diverticulum typically affects elderly patients (>60 years) and can be diagnosed definitively with barium swallow imaging.


• The investigation of choice is barium swallow (lateral X-ray).

• It is treated with:
• Cricopharyngeal myotomy
• Surgical diverticulectomy
• Marsupialization
• Dohlmann's procedure

Other Options:
Option A: Achalasia presents with dysphagia for both solids and liquids but no foul breath or posterior pharyngeal pouch.
Option C: Tracheoesophageal fistula usually presents in neonates; acquired cases in adults are rare and associated with recurrent aspiration and cough.
Option D: Carcinoma esophagus presents with progressive dysphagia, weight loss, and sometimes odynophagia, without a pharyngeal pouch on barium swallow.

stem: Truelove and Witts classification is used for?
subject: Medicine, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The Truelove and Witts classification is used to assess the severity of ulcerative colitis, particularly acute severe flares.
• It stratifies patients based on stool frequency, presence of blood in stool, fever, tachycardia, anemia, and ESR.
• This classification helps guide management decisions, such as hospitalization, corticosteroid therapy, or escalation to immunosuppressive therapy.
• Mild colitis: <4 stools/day, minimal blood, no systemic toxicity.
• Moderate colitis: 4-6 stools/day, mild systemic symptoms.
• Severe colitis: >=6 bloody stools/day plus systemic toxicity (fever >37.8°C, tachycardia >90/min, anemia, ESR >30 mm/hr).


ParameterMildModerateSevere
Bowel movements< 4 per day4-6 per day> 6 per day
Blood in stoolSmallModerateSevere
FeverNone< 37.5°C> 37.5°C
TachycardiaNone< 90 mean pulse> 90 mean pulse
AnemiaMildHb >= 75% of normalHb < 75% of normal
ESR< 30 mm< 30 mm> 30 mm
Endoscopic appearanceErythema Decreased vascular markings Fine granularityMarked erythema Absent vascular markings Coarse granularity Contact bleeding No ulcerationsSpontaneous bleeding Ulcerations

Other Options:
Option A: Acute encephalitis severity is not classified by Truelove and Witts.
Option B: Acute pancreatitis uses Ranson, BISAP, or Atlanta criteria for severity.
Option C: ARDS severity is classified by Berlin definition (PaO2/FiO2 ratio).

stem: Inhibitors of Poly ADP ribose polymerase (PARP) are approved for BRCA-positive breast cancers. Which among the following drugs is used?
subject: Pharmacology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Rucaparib is a PARP inhibitor approved for BRCA-positive breast and advanced ovarian cancers.
• PARP is a nuclear enzyme that catalyzes ADP-ribosylation using NAD+ and plays a key role in DNA damage repair.
• Other PARP inhibitors approved for breast cancer include: 1. Olaparib 2. Talazoparib.
• PARP inhibitors are particularly effective in tumors with BRCA1/2 mutations, exploiting synthetic lethality to induce cancer cell death.


Other Options:
Option A: Palbociclib is a CDK4/6 inhibitor, used in metastatic hormone receptor-positive breast cancer.
Option C: Regorafenib is an angiogenesis inhibitor indicated for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma.
Option D: Vandetanib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor used for unresectable medullary thyroid carcinoma.

stem: A 60-year-old patient who is a known case of hypertension and diabetes underwent PCI stent placement for NSTEMI last year. He is currently on aspirin, metoprolol and atorvastatin. He comes for a follow-up, and his lipid profile is as follows: Total cholesterol 252mg/dl, LDL: 156mg/dl, triglyceride 158mg/dl and HDL: 40 mg/dL. Which drugs can be added based on his lipid profile? 1. Ezetimibe 2. Evolocumab 3. Gemfibrozil 4. Bempedoic acid
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The patient has atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) due to prior NSTEMI and PCI. The treatment goal is maximizing LDL reduction.
• Ezetimibe inhibits intestinal cholesterol absorption, lowering LDL and total cholesterol.
• Evolocumab is a PCSK9 inhibitor, which prevents LDL receptor degradation, enhancing LDL clearance.
• Bempedoic acid inhibits ATP-citrate lyase (ACL), decreasing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering LDL.
• Gemfibrozil is a fibrate that primarily reduces triglycerides, with minimal effect on LDL, and is not preferred for ASCVD LDL reduction.
• Combining statins with ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, or bempedoic acid has been shown to reduce cardiovascular risk, whereas fibrates and niacin do not significantly reduce ASCVD events when added to statins.


Other Options:
Option B: Excludes bempedoic acid, which is appropriate in statin-intolerant or insufficient response patients.
Option C: Includes gemfibrozil, which does not significantly reduce LDL in this context.
Option D: Excludes Evolocumab, missing a potent LDL-lowering agent for high-risk ASCVD patients.

stem: Which of the following is a true statement regarding Pretomanid?
options: Given in XDR TB with Bedaquiline and Linezolid
Contraindicated in patients on enzyme inducer drugs
subject: Pharmacology, Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Pretomanid is a bicyclic nitroimidazole used in the BPaL regimen (Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid) for pre-XDR and treatment-intolerant MDR-TB.
• It is contraindicated with CYP3A enzyme inducers (e.g., rifampicin, phenytoin, carbamazepine) as they reduce drug levels and compromise efficacy.
• Its mechanism involves generation of reactive nitrogen species, inhibiting mycolic acid synthesis and disrupting bacterial protein synthesis.
• Not indicated for malaria.
• Key exam point: contraindication with enzyme inducers is critical to remember.
Type of TB CaseIntensive PhaseContinuation Phase

H mono-resistant / poly-drug resistant TBLfx REZ for 6 months6 months long. Oral drugs only.

Other Options:
Option A: Pretomanid is used with Bedaquiline and Linezolid, but the key specific correct fact highlighted in the context is contraindication with enzyme inducers.
Option C: It acts via reactive nitrogen species, not oxygen species.
Option D: Not used for malaria.

stem: Which of the following drugs act on sodium channels and potentiate slow sodium inactivation?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Lacosamide selectively enhances slow inactivation of voltage-gated sodium channels, stabilizing hyperexcitable neuronal membranes and inhibiting repetitive firing.
• Unlike traditional sodium channel blockers (e.g., phenytoin, carbamazepine), lacosamide does not affect fast inactivation, reducing risk of generalized suppression of normal neuronal activity.
• It also binds to CRMP-2 (collapsing response mediator protein-2), though the role in seizure control is not fully defined.
• Approved for monotherapy or adjunctive therapy in focal-onset seizures in patients >= 17 years.
• Available in oral and IV formulations, minimal hepatic metabolism, and safe in patients with respiratory compromise.
Antiepileptic DrugsMechanism of Action
PhenytoinInhibit Na channels
CarbamazepineInhibit Na channels
LamotrigineInhibit Na channels
LacosamideEnhances slow inactivation of voltage-gated Na channels


Other Options:
Option A: Phenytoin acts via fast sodium channel inactivation, not slow inactivation.
Option B: Lamotrigine primarily inhibits fast sodium channels and reduces glutamate release.
Option D: Topiramate has multiple mechanisms (enhances GABA, blocks AMPA/kainate receptors, inhibits carbonic anhydrase) but does not specifically potentiate slow sodium inactivation.

stem: Which drug acts on the marked receptor?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The marked receptor is GABA transporter-1 (GAT-1), responsible for reuptake of GABA from the synaptic cleft.
• Tiagabine inhibits GAT-1, prolonging GABA action in the synaptic cleft and enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission.
• Other drugs acting on the GABAergic system include: 1. Activation of GABA-A receptors: Barbiturates, Phenobarbitone 2. Activation of GABA-B receptors: Baclofen 3. Prevents degradation of GABA (GABA transaminase inhibitors): Vigabatrin.
• Tiagabine is primarily used as an adjunctive therapy for focal-onset seizures.


Other Options:
Option B: Ganaxolone is a GABA-A receptor modulator (positive allosteric).
Option C: Felbamate acts mainly as an NMDA receptor antagonist and GABA-A modulator.
Option D: Stiripentol enhances GABA-A receptor activity but does not block GAT-1.

stem:
Match the treatment with its respective condition:

Column AColumn B
a. Naloxonei. Smoking cessation
b. Oxazepamii. Opioid overdose
c. Vareniclineiii. Delirium tremens
d. Acamprosateiv. Treatment of alcoholism
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Naloxone: Opioid antagonist used to reverse opioid overdose (respiratory depression, sedation).
• Oxazepam: Benzodiazepine used in delirium tremens and severe alcohol withdrawal.
• Varenicline: Partial agonist at nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, used for smoking cessation by reducing cravings and withdrawal symptoms.
• Acamprosate: Modulates glutamate and GABA neurotransmission, used in treatment of alcoholism to reduce cravings and risk of relapse.


Other Options:
Option A, C, D: Incorrect as the drugs are mismatched with their respective conditions.

stem: A young female weighing 46 kg is on digoxin with Vd=6 L/kg and clearance = 4.6 L/h. Calculate the half-life of digoxin:
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The half-life of Digoxin is 42 hours.
• Digoxin exhibits first-order kinetics. In first-order kinetics, the half-life can be calculated as follows:
• The volume of distribution (Vd) is often expressed per kilogram (e.g., 6 L/kg) to standardize it across patients of different sizes. Here, the Vd is given as 6 L/kg. However, to be used in the above equation, Vd has to be expressed in litres.
• Thus, the Vd in this patient will be:
• Step 1: Convert Vd to litres: Vd = 6 L/kg x 46 kg = 276 L
• Step 2: Calculate half-life: t 1/2 = 0.693 x (276 / 4.6) = 41.58 ~ 42 hours.


Other Options:
Option A: 4 h - Incorrect, too short; does not account for digoxin's large Vd.
Option B: 68 h - Incorrect, overestimation; CL is not that low.
Option D: 0.9 h - Incorrect, extremely short, not physiologically plausible.

stem: Choose the correct option regarding phenytoin:
options: Acute oral overdose results primarily in cerebellar and vestibular symptoms and high doses have been associated with marked cerebellar atrophy
High dose causes cardiotoxicity in old and nephrotoxicity in young patients
These complications can be minimized by administering fosphenytoin at a rate of less than 250 mg of phenytoin sodium equivalents per minute
Given in all patients with seizures regardless of cardiac arrhythmias, hypotension and/or CNS depression
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Acute oral phenytoin overdose primarily affects the cerebellum and vestibular system.
• Common manifestations include ataxia, vertigo, nystagmus, and diplopia.
• Chronic high doses of phenytoin can lead to cerebellar atrophy.
• Toxicity is dose-dependent and more pronounced with prolonged high plasma concentrations.
• IV administration carries additional risk of cardiac complications, but oral overdose mainly causes neurological symptoms.


Other Options:
Option B: Cardiotoxicity occurs primarily with rapid IV administration, not oral overdose; nephrotoxicity is not typical.
Option C: Safe IV fosphenytoin infusion rate is <=150 mg phenytoin equivalents per minute, not 250 mg/min.
Option D: Phenytoin is ineffective for absence seizures and is contraindicated in certain seizure types with cardiac instability or CNS depression.

stem: True statement about Iron Isomaltoside 1000:
options: Iron requirement can be fulfilled in single dose
Given intravenously and gets deposited in adipose tissue therefore less toxic
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Iron isomaltoside 1000 allows complete iron replacement in a single intravenous dose.
• It is used in adults intolerant to oral iron or in CKD patients not on hemodialysis.
• The slow release of iron from the complex reduces toxicity.
• Can be safely administered with erythropoietin to support erythropoiesis.
• It has a low immunogenic profile compared to older formulations like iron dextran.


Other Options:
Option B: Not more immunogenic than iron dextran; actually safer.
Option C: Reduced toxicity is due to controlled iron release, not deposition in adipose tissue.
Option D: Can be safely combined with erythropoietin therapy.

stem: The drug that binds to CD4 and has action on both CCR5 and CXCR4 in tropic HIV infections is:
subject: Pharmacology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Ibalizumab is a monoclonal antibody targeting CD4 receptors on T-helper cells.
• It acts on both CCR5- and CXCR4-tropic HIV-1 strains, preventing viral entry.
• Functions as a post-attachment entry inhibitor, blocking steps required for fusion and viral transmission.
• Administered intravenously for multidrug-resistant HIV-1.
• Prevents infection of CD4+ T cells without causing immunosuppression.
• It does not interfere with host CCR5/CXCR4 signaling directly, only blocks HIV entry.


Other Options:
Option A: Maraviroc targets CCR5 only; does not bind CD4.
Option C: Elvitegravir is an integrase inhibitor, preventing proviral DNA integration.
Option D: Fosamprenavir is a protease inhibitor, blocking viral maturation.

stem: A 60-year-old male recovering from facial nerve palsy complains of food getting stuck between the cheek and teeth while eating. What is the muscle affected in this case?
subject: Anatomy, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• The patient's complaint of food getting stuck between the cheek and teeth indicates dysfunction of the buccinator muscle.
• Buccinator is a muscle of facial expression, innervated by the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII).
• It helps keep food between the teeth during mastication by pressing the cheeks against the teeth.
• Paralysis due to facial nerve palsy leads to accumulation of food in the buccal pouch, as the muscle cannot contract effectively.
• This muscle is also known as the 'whistling muscle', contributing to actions like blowing and sucking.


Other Options:
Option B: Levator labii superioris elevates the upper lip and dilates the nostrils; it does not keep food in place.
Option C: Zygomaticus major elevates the angle of the mouth (smiling) but does not affect mastication.
Option D: Orbicularis oris closes the lips and assists in speech and chewing, but its paralysis does not lead to food accumulation in the cheek.

stem: Identify the marked structure:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The superficial temporal artery is a terminal branch of the external carotid artery.
• It ascends in front of the ear, passing over the zygomatic arch.
• Travels superficially, supplying the scalp and temple region.
• Often palpable just above the zygomatic arch and anterior to the ear.
• It runs parallel to the auriculotemporal nerve and is clinically important in temporal arteritis.


Other Options:
Option B: Posterior auricular artery is smaller, located behind the ear, supplying the auricle and scalp.
Option C: Middle meningeal artery is deep within the cranial cavity, supplying the meninges; not visible superficially.
Option D: Occipital artery arises posteriorly from the external carotid and supplies the posterior scalp.

stem: The marked structure serves as a surface landmark for which anatomical structure?
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The marked structure is the pterion, formed by the junction of frontal, parietal, temporal, and sphenoid bones.
• It serves as a surface landmark for the stem of the lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure), which separates the frontal and temporal lobes.
• Deep to the lateral sulcus lies the insular cortex and portions of the basal ganglia (e.g., putamen).
• The middle cerebral artery and its branches lie beneath the pterion within the lateral sulcus.
• Clinically, fractures at the pterion can rupture the middle meningeal artery, causing an epidural hematoma.
• Neurosurgically, the pterion is often used as a site for trephination.


Other Options:
Option A: Pineal gland is located posteriorly near the midline, deep in the cranial cavity.
Option B: Wernicke's area lies in the posterior superior temporal gyrus of the dominant hemisphere.
Option D: Internal carotid artery siphon is the S-shaped course of ICA through the cavernous sinus, unrelated to the pterion.

stem: Which of the following is not a derivative of neural crest cells?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) is derived from the neuroectoderm (outer layer of the optic cup), not neural crest cells.
• Neural crest cells (NCCs) migrate from the dorsal neural tube and give rise to multiple structures in both cranial and trunk regions.
• Cranial NCC derivatives include: 1. Ciliary muscles 2. Ciliary ganglion 3. Craniofacial skeleton 4. Melanocytes 5. Satellite glial cells.
• Trunk NCC derivatives include: 1. Sensory ganglia 2. Sympathetic and enteric neurons 3. Schwann cells 4. Adrenal medulla cells.
• Additional derivatives: C cells of thyroid, conotruncal septum of heart, odontoblasts, and facial/forebrain vascular smooth muscle.
NeuralMesenchymal
All ganglionsConnective tissue and bones of the face and skull
All enteric plexusDermis of the head and face
Schwann cells and Satellite glial cellsOdontoblasts (Dentine)
Adrenal MedullaArachnoid and Piamater
Parafollicular C cells of ThyroidConotruncal septum in the heart
Melanoblast/Melanocytes (except Retinal Pigment Epithelium)


Other Options:
Option A: Ciliary muscles are derived from cranial NCCs.
Option C: Ciliary ganglion neurons arise from cranial NCCs.
Option D: Melanocytes originate from neural crest cells.

stem: The cranial component of the neural crest cells leads to the formation of?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Cranial neural crest cells contribute to the septation of the outflow tract of the heart, forming the conotruncal (aortopulmonary) septum.
• The conotruncal septum divides the truncus arteriosus into the aorta and pulmonary trunk.
• Defective formation of conotruncal septum can lead to persistent truncus arteriosus or other conotruncal defects.
• Heart development involves multiple components: 1. First heart field: Left ventricle and part of atria 2. Second heart field: Right ventricle, outflow tract, part of atria 3. Neural crest cells: Conotruncal septum and outflow tract septation 4. Endocardial cushions: AV valves and septa.
• Cranial NCCs migrate into the truncus arteriosus to guide proper rotation and alignment of the outflow tract.
NeuralMesenchymal
All ganglionsConnective tissue and bones of the face and skull
All enteric plexusDermis of the head and face
Schwann cells and Satellite glial cellsOdontoblasts (Dentine)
Adrenal MedullaArachnoid and Piamater
Parafollicular C cells of ThyroidConotruncal septum in the heart
Melanoblast/Melanocytes (except Retinal Pigment Epithelium)


Other Options:
Option A: SA node arises from the sinus venosus (primary heart field).
Option B: AV cushions are formed from endocardial cells and mesenchyme, not neural crest.
Option D: Chordae tendineae develop from mesenchymal tissue within the heart.

stem: A 15-year-old boy underwent splenectomy for immune thrombocytopenic purpura. During the procedure, the surgeon noticed bleeding while separating the spleen from the stomach. Which vessels are most likely implicated in this bleeding?
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The spleen is attached to the stomach via the gastrosplenic ligament.
• The gastrosplenic ligament contains short gastric vessels and left gastroepiploic vessels.
• During splenectomy, when the spleen is separated from the stomach, short gastric vessels are the most commonly injured vessels, leading to bleeding.
• Careful ligation of short gastric vessels is essential to prevent hemorrhage.
• Right gastroepiploic vessels and left gastric vessels do not run in the gastrosplenic ligament.
• Splenic vessels run along the superior border of the pancreas and are not typically injured while separating the spleen from the stomach.


Peritoneal foldContent
Lesser omentum (a. Hepatoduodenal ligament)Hepatic artery, portal vein, and common bile duct
Lesser omentum (b. Hepatogastric ligament)Right and left gastric vessels
Greater omentumRight and left gastro-epiploic vessels
Gastrosplenic ligamentShort gastric and left gastro-epiploic branches of splenic artery
Splenorenal/Lienorenal ligamentSplenic vessels. Also contains the tail of the pancreas


Other Options:
Option A: Left gastric vessels run along the lesser curvature of the stomach; unlikely to be injured here.
Option C: Right gastroepiploic vessels run along the inferior greater curvature not part of the gastrosplenic ligament.
Option D: Splenic vessels lie along the pancreatic border: not involved in this step of surgery.

stem: Identify the marked structure:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The primitive streak appears in early gastrulation on the dorsal surface of the epiblast in the caudal region of the bilaminar embryonic disc.
• It establishes the body axes: cranio-caudal and left-right orientation.
• It serves as the site for cell migration to form the mesoderm and endoderm.
• Marks the beginning of gastrulation, transforming the epiblast into a trilaminar disc.
• Its formation precedes the development of the notochord and neural tube.




Other Options:
Option B: Notochord is a mesodermal rod forming after the primitive streak, important for neural tube induction.
Option C: Neural groove forms from the ectoderm during neurulation, after the primitive streak appears.
Option D: Secondary neural plate is involved in later stages of neural tube formation, not present at the primitive streak stage.

stem: Which of the following is the first to be formed by the migration of the epiblast along the primitive streak?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• During gastrulation, epiblast cells migrate through the primitive streak to form the three germ layers.
• The first migrating epiblast cells displace the hypoblast to form the definitive endoderm.
• Endoderm gives rise to the gastrointestinal tract, liver, and lungs.
• Subsequent migrating cells form the mesoderm, giving rise to muscles, bones, and cardiovascular structures.
• Remaining epiblast cells that do not migrate form the ectoderm, which becomes the epidermis and nervous system.


Other Options:
Option B: Cardiac mesoderm arises from the mesoderm, formed after endoderm.
Option C: Epidermis arises from ectoderm, which is the last layer to be established.
Option D: Neural groove develops from ectoderm during neurulation, after mesoderm formation.

stem: During walking, the hip bone on the side of the suspended leg is raised by the action of which muscle present in the supporting leg?
subject: Anatomy, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Gluteus medius of the supporting leg stabilizes the pelvis during walking and prevents the hip on the side of the suspended leg from dropping.
• It acts as a hip abductor and is assisted by gluteus minimus.
• Function is clinically tested by asking the patient to stand on one leg.
• Weakness or paralysis leads to pelvic tilt toward the lifted leg, known as a positive Trendelenburg sign.
• These muscles ensure smooth gait and prevent lateral swaying of the trunk.


Other Options:
Option B: Gluteus maximus extends and externally rotates the hip; important for climbing or rising from sitting, not for stabilizing the pelvis in single-leg stance.
Option C: Obturator internus externally rotates and abducts the hip when flexed, does not prevent pelvic drop.
Option D: Quadratus femoris externally rotates and adducts the hip; does not elevate the hip during gait.

stem: Identify the marked structure:
subject: Anatomy, Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• The oculomotor nerve (CN III) passes through the superior orbital fissure to enter the orbit.
• It lies in the lateral wall of the cavernous sinus, superior to the trochlear nerve and ophthalmic division of CN V.
• CN III innervates most extraocular muscles (superior, medial, and inferior rectus; inferior oblique) and levator palpebrae superioris, along with parasympathetic fibers to the pupil.
• Lesions of CN III can cause ptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and pupillary dilation.
• Distinction from nearby nerves, CN IV is smaller and superior-lateral, CN VI is medial and in the sinus, and V1 lies below CN III in the lateral wall.






Other Options:
Option B: Abducens nerve (CN VI) innervates lateral rectus medial path in sinus.
Option C: Trochlear nerve (CN IV) innervates superior oblique follows a higher and lateral path.
Option D: Ophthalmic division (V1) of trigeminal nerve carries sensory fibers located below CN III in the lateral wall.

stem: Injury to the posterior interosseous nerve leads to?
options: Loss of extension of the wrist
subject: Anatomy, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• The posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) is the terminal motor branch of the radial nerve.
• It passes through the supinator muscle and supplies extensor muscles of the forearm, except extensor carpi radialis longus and brachioradialis.
• Injury leads to inability to extend the fingers at the MCP joints, causing loss of digit extension.
• Wrist extension is partially preserved due to intact extensor carpi radialis longus.
• Supination is preserved because supinator and biceps brachii can still act.
• Clinically, PIN injury presents with finger drop, but no complete wrist drop.


Other Options:
Option B: Loss of extension of the wrist implies a higher radial nerve lesion.
Option C: Loss of supination is not fully lost as biceps brachii is a strong supinator.
Option D: Wrist drop occurs with radial nerve injury above the elbow.

stem: Nutritional deficiency of copper, required by lysyl oxidase which is an important key step for formation of covalent cross-linking in collagen synthesis, gives rise to which of the following conditions?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Menke's disease is caused by a mutation in the ATP7A gene, leading to impaired intestinal absorption and transport of copper.
• Copper is a cofactor for lysyl oxidase, which catalyzes covalent cross-linking in collagen, essential for collagen strength and rigidity.
• Deficiency of copper results in weak, brittle connective tissue, causing skeletal abnormalities and multiple fractures.
• Clinical features include kinky, depigmented hair, neurodegeneration (truncal hypotonia, developmental delay, seizures), failure to thrive, anemia, neutropenia, and recurrent infections.
• The collagen defect due to lysyl oxidase deficiency differentiates it from vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) or genetic collagen disorders (Ehlers-Danlos).
StepDetails
1. Collagen backbonePreprocollagen Glycin-proline-lysine
2. HydroxylationOf proline and lysine in the presence of Vit C. Deficiency of Vit C causes Scurvy.
3. GlycosylationFormation of procollagen and resulting triple helix
4. Cleavage of terminals of procollagenResulting in the formation of tropocollagen
5. Cross-linking by lysyl oxidase (copper-containing enzyme)Formation of mature collagen. Defect causes Menkes disease and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.



Other Options:
Option B: Scurvy arises from vitamin C deficiency, impairing hydroxylation of proline and lysine in collagen.
Option C: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome involves genetic defects in collagen structure or synthesis, not copper metabolism.
Option D: Scoliosis is a structural spinal deformity, not related to copper deficiency.

stem:
Match the following with appropriate precursors:
Column AColumn B
i. Melatonina. Tyrosine
ii. Histamineb. Tryptophan
iii. Norepinephrinec. Cystine
iv. Glutathioned. Histidine
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Melatonin is synthesized from tryptophan, first converted into serotonin and then into melatonin by the pineal gland.
• Histamine is derived from histidine, catalyzed by histidine decarboxylase, and functions as a neurotransmitter and regulator of gastric acid.
• Norepinephrine is synthesized from tyrosine, which is converted to L-DOPA, then dopamine, and finally hydroxylated to norepinephrine.
• Glutathione is a tripeptide composed of glutamate, cysteine (from cystine), and glycine, acting as a major intracellular antioxidant.


Other Options:
Option B: Melatonin cannot be derived from cystine and glutathione is not synthesized from tryptophan.
Option C: Melatonin is not derived from tyrosine and norepinephrine is not synthesized from tryptophan.
Option D: Histamine is not derived from tryptophan and glutathione is not derived from tyrosine.

stem: In the current scenario treatment for urea cycle disorder is available. Administration of amino acid phenylbutyrate can decrease ammonia formation. Which amino acid provides ammonia to phenylbutyrate?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Glutamate provides ammonia to phenylbutyrate in urea cycle disorder management.
• Ammonia is converted to glutamate via transamination and transported to the liver as glutamine.
• Phenylbutyrate is metabolized to phenylacetate, which conjugates with glutamine to form phenylacetylglutamine, a water-soluble compound excreted in urine.
• This process bypasses the defective urea cycle and removes excess nitrogen from the body.
• Glutamine is derived from glutamate, making glutamate the amino acid providing ammonia in this pathway.


Other Options:
Option A & C: Arginine and aspartate do not contribute ammonia to phenylbutyrate in this reaction.
Option D: Glycine combines with benzoate (not phenylbutyrate) to form hippuric acid, another nitrogen-scavenging mechanism.

stem:
Which among the following are appropriate techniques for chromosomal aneuploidy?
NumberTechnique
i.In situ hybridization
ii.Conventional cytogenetics
iii.Sanger sequencing
iv.Polymerase chain reaction
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• In situ hybridization (e.g., FISH) uses fluorescent probes to detect specific chromosome sequences and can identify numerical abnormalities such as aneuploidy.
• Conventional cytogenetics (karyotyping) visually assesses chromosome number and structure, making it a standard for detecting aneuploidy.
• Sanger sequencing, when combined with high-throughput analysis and interpretation of copy number variation, can contribute to identifying aneuploidy in the context of genome analysis.
• Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) techniques like quantitative fluorescent PCR (QF-PCR) are established in rapid aneuploidy detection by amplifying chromosome-specific sequences.


Other Options:
Option B: Omit molecular methods like Sanger sequencing and PCR that can be adapted for aneuploidy detection.
Option C: Excludes in situ hybridization and PCR, both useful for detecting chromosomal copy number changes.
Option D: Excludes cytogenetic and hybridization techniques, which are foundational methods for aneuploidy analysis.

stem:
Enzymes used in recombinant DNA technology are?
1. Isomerase
2. Phosphatase
3. Terminal transferase
4. Crispr Cas9
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Phosphatase removes phosphate groups from the 5' ends of DNA, preventing self-ligation and enabling controlled ligation of DNA fragments.
• Terminal transferase adds nucleotides to the 3' end of DNA molecules, useful for homopolymer tailing and generating sticky ends for recombinant DNA construction.
• CRISPR-Cas9 is an RNA-guided endonuclease system that introduces site-specific double-stranded DNA breaks, facilitating targeted genome editing.
• These enzymes are essential tools in recombinant DNA technology for producing therapeutic proteins, vaccines, and for functional genomic studies.
• Isomerases are not used in recombinant DNA technology as they catalyze structural rearrangements of molecules, not DNA modification.
EnzymesUse
PhosphatasesRemoval of 5-PO4 groups prior to kinase labeling; also used to prevent self-ligation
DNA ligaseJoining of DNA molecules
DNA polymerase ISynthesis of double-stranded cDNA; nick translation; generation of blunt ends from sticky ends
Thermostable DNA polymerasesPolymerase chain reaction (DNA synthesis)
DNAse INick translation; mapping of hypersensitive sites; mapping protein-DNA interactions
Exonuclease IIIDNA sequencing; ChIPexo, mapping of DNA-protein interactions
A. ExonucleaseDNA sequencing
Polynucleotide kinase32P end-labeling of DNA or RNA
Reverse transcriptaseSynthesis of cDNA from mRNA
RNase HRNA (5' end) mapping studies
S1 nucleaseRemoval of hairpin in synthesis of cDNA; RNA mapping studies (both 5' and 3' ends)
Terminal transferaseHomopolymer tailing
Recombinases (CRE, INT, FLP)Generation of specific chimeric DNA molecules, work both in vitro and in vivo



Other Options:
Option A: Incorrect because it includes Isomerase, which is not used in recombinant DNA technology.
Option B: Incorrect because it excludes CRISPR-Cas9, a key modern gene-editing tool.
Option D: Incorrect because it excludes phosphatase, essential for preventing self-ligation of DNA fragments.

stem: In a patient with diabetes mellitus, the liver produces more TG and VLDL. What is the cause of the same?
options: Decreased action of LPL with increased action of HSL
Decreased action of LPL with Decreased action of HSL
Increased action of LPL with increased action of HSL
Increased action of LPL with decreased action of HSL
subject: Biochemistry, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Lipoprotein lipase (LPL) hydrolyzes triglycerides in VLDL and chylomicrons into free fatty acids for tissue uptake; its activity is induced by insulin.
• In diabetes mellitus, insulin deficiency or resistance leads to decreased LPL activity, causing accumulation of TG and VLDL in the blood.
• Hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL) breaks down stored triglycerides in adipocytes into free fatty acids and glycerol; insulin normally inhibits HSL.
• Low insulin in diabetes results in increased HSL activity, promoting lipolysis and release of fatty acids to the liver for VLDL synthesis.
• The combination of decreased LPL and increased HSL results in hypertriglyceridemia and elevated VLDL production in the liver.
FeatureHSLLipoprotein lipase
LocationAdipocyteCapillaries
ActionHydrolyse TAG in adipose tissueHydrolyse TAG in chylomicron, VLDL
Acts in the state ofFasting (Glucagon)Fed (Insulin)



Other Options:
Option B: Incorrect because HSL would be increased, not decreased, in diabetes.
Option C: Incorrect because LPL is not increased in diabetes; it is decreased due to low insulin.
Option D: Incorrect because LPL is decreased, not increased, in diabetes.

stem:
Match the following hormones with their receptors:
HormoneReceptor
A. Insulin1. JAK STAT pathway
B. Acetylcholine2. Nuclear receptor
C. Growth hormone3. Tyrosine kinase receptor
D. Thyroxine4. Ligand gated ion channel
subject: Physiology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Insulin - Tyrosine kinase receptor (3): Insulin binds to a receptor with intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity. Receptor activation causes autophosphorylation and downstream signaling through MAP kinase and PI3 kinase pathways, leading to glucose uptake and metabolic regulation.
• Acetylcholine - Ligand gated ion channel (4): Acetylcholine acts on nicotinic receptors, which are ligand gated ion channels. Binding opens sodium and potassium channels, producing rapid synaptic transmission.
• Growth hormone - JAK STAT pathway (1): Growth hormone binds to a receptor without intrinsic kinase activity. It activates Janus kinase, which phosphorylates STAT proteins, leading to gene transcription and growth related effects.
• Thyroxine - Nuclear receptor (2): Thyroxine enters the cell and binds to nuclear receptors. The hormone receptor complex directly regulates gene transcription and protein synthesis.


Other Options:
Option B: Incorrect pairing of insulin and growth hormone receptors.
Option C: Thyroxine does not act via ligand gated channels, and growth hormone does not use nuclear receptors.
Option D: Multiple mismatches including thyroxine incorrectly paired with tyrosine kinase receptor.

stem: The O2 saturation with respect to PO2 is shown by the curve marked as 5. What happens to the oxygen-Hb dissociation curve on transfusion of stored RBCs?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Transfusion of stored RBCs causes the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve to shift left, while maintaining its sigmoid shape, as represented by curve 2. This occurs due to reduced 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG) levels in stored RBCs.
• 2,3-BPG normally binds hemoglobin and decreases its oxygen affinity, facilitating oxygen release to tissues.
• During storage, RBC metabolism slows, leading to depletion of 2,3-BPG, which increases hemoglobin's oxygen affinity.
• As a result, hemoglobin holds oxygen more tightly, and oxygen delivery to tissues is transiently reduced immediately after transfusion.


Other Options:
Option B and C: Incorrect because they indicate a right shift, which occurs with increased 2,3-BPG (e.g. chronic hypoxia, anaerobic glycolysis). Stored RBCs have depleted 2,3-BPG, so a right shift does not occur.
Option D: Incorrect because it does not represent the expected left shift after storage.

stem: Not seen in decompression sickness:
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Hemodilution is not a feature of decompression sickness; instead, hemoconcentration occurs due to fluid shifts.
• Nitrogen bubbles form when a diver rapidly ascends from high pressure, escaping from tissues and blood, which can block small vessels and cause tissue ischemia.
• These bubbles lead to venous stasis and third-spacing of fluids, increasing blood viscosity rather than diluting it.
• Clinical manifestations include cutaneous signs like erythematous or reticular rashes (cutis marmorata), and systemic symptoms such as limb pain, dyspnea, neurological deficits, visual disturbances, and hearing loss.
• Vascular obstruction by bubbles can also trigger coagulopathy due to endothelial damage and clotting activation.


Other Options:
Option A: Vision loss can occur due to bubbles affecting the optic nerve or retinal vessels.
Option B: Hearing loss or tinnitus may result from bubbles affecting the auditory system.
Option D: Coagulopathy can develop secondary to bubble-induced vascular injury and disrupted clotting.

stem: Which of the following ovarian structures is responsible for the following finding?
subject: Physiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The corpus luteum is responsible for the secretory phase of the endometrium, which is characterized histologically by coiled, corkscrew-shaped glands filled with glycogen-rich secretions.
• It secretes progesterone, which transforms the endometrium from the proliferative to the secretory phase, preparing it for potential implantation.
• Proliferative phase: Glands are straight, tubular, lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium, with dense stroma and mitotic activity.
• Early secretory phase: Glands begin to coil, subnuclear vacuoles appear due to glycogen accumulation in epithelial cells.
• Late secretory phase: Glands become maximally tortuous with 'saw-tooth' morphology; stroma becomes edematous, and glandular secretions peak.
• Recognizing these changes is crucial for correlating ovarian hormonal function with endometrial histology.










Other Options:
Option A: Secondary follicle is an earlier stage of follicular development, containing an antrum and theca layers, not responsible for secretory-phase endometrial changes.
Option B: Theca interna consists of endocrine stromal cells that produce androgens for estrogen synthesis but do not directly induce secretory-phase changes.
Option C: Granulosa cells line the follicle, support oocyte maturation, and produce estrogen but are not directly responsible for progesterone-driven secretory endometrial changes.

stem: Which of the following does not form the inner component of photoreceptors?
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Membranous discs are part of the outer segment of photoreceptors, containing photopigments such as rhodopsin in rods and photopsins in cones, which are essential for light detection.
• The inner segment of photoreceptors houses the cellular organelles responsible for metabolism and protein synthesis, including mitochondria, Golgi apparatus, and endoplasmic reticulum.
• The inner segment provides energy and biosynthetic support to maintain the outer segment and overall photoreceptor function. Distinguishing between the inner and outer segments is critical for understanding phototransduction and retinal physiology.




Other Options:
Option A: Mitochondria are located in the inner segment, supplying ATP for photoreceptor activity.
Option C: Golgi apparatus in the inner segment participates in protein processing for the photopigments.
Option D: Endoplasmic reticulum in the inner segment is involved in protein synthesis and folding necessary for photoreceptor maintenance.

stem: Which of the following does not stimulate catecholamine synthesis?
subject: Physiology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Hyperglycemia does not stimulate catecholamine synthesis; instead, it suppresses sympathetic activity. Catecholamine release is primarily triggered by hypoglycemia.
• Smoking activates nicotinic receptors in autonomic ganglia and adrenal medulla, Increasing catecholamine release.
• Standing posture causes a transient fall in venous return, activating baroreceptors and sympathetic outflow, thereby Increasing catecholamine secretion.
• Exercise increases sympathetic nervous system activity to meet metabolic and cardiovascular demands, leading to increased catecholamine synthesis.


Other Options:
Option B: Smoking stimulates catecholamine release via sympathetic activation.
Option C: Postural change activates baroreceptor-mediated sympathetic response.
Option D: Exercise enhances catecholamine synthesis to support increased metabolic demand.

stem: While collecting a blood sample for serum calcium estimation, which of the following should be avoided to ensure accurate measurement?
options: If a tourniquet is needed, tie and release as soon as the sample is collected
Not collecting samples from patients immobilized for long periods
subject: Biochemistry, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Exercise Increases sympathetic activity, leading to transient changes in blood pH and calcium binding.
• Catecholamine release during exercise alters lonized calcium levels by shifting calcium binding to plasma proteins.
• This results in falsely altered serum calcium values, making post-exercise samples unreliable.
• For accurate estimation, blood should be drawn when the patient is at rest and metabolically stable.


Other Options:
Option A: Prolonged tourniquet application causes hemoconcentration; hence it should be released early, making this a correct practice.
Option C: Fist clenching increases local muscular activity and may falsely elevate lonized calcium levels.
Option D: Prolonged immobilization increases bone resorption and serum calcium; samples from such patients may not reflect baseline levels.

stem: Which receptor contributes to static two-point discrimination?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Merkel cells are slowly adapting mechanoreceptors responsible for detecting static touch, pressure, and fine spatial details.
• They play a key role in static two-point discrimination, allowing precise localization of tactile stimuli.
• Merkel cells are located in the basal layer of the epidermis, especially in glabrous skin such as fingertips.
• They respond continuously to sustained pressure, making them ideal for detecting shape, edges, and texture.
• In contrast, rapidly adapting receptors respond to movement rather than static touch.
ReceptorMerkel's discMeissner corpuscleRuffini end organPacinian corpuscle

AdaptationSlowRapidSlowRapid

Examples: Merkel's disc. Ruffini's end organs. Joint capsule, Muscle spindle, Golgi tendon organsExamples: Pacinian corpuscle, Meissner's corpuscles, Hair end organs


Other Options:
Option B: Meissner corpuscles are rapidly adapting receptors responsible for dynamic two-point discrimination, not static.
Option C: Pacinian corpuscles detect vibration and deep pressure, not fine spatial discrimination.
Option D: Incorrect because only Merkel cells mediate static two-point discrimination.

stem: Mitochondrial sheaths are found in which part of the sperm?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The middle plece of the sperm lies between the head and the principal piece of the tail.
• It contains a spiral arrangement of mitochondria, forming the mitochondrial sheath around the central axoneme.
• These mitochondria produce ATP, which powers flagellar movement and enables sperm motility.
• The mitochondrial sheath is essential for sustained swimming and energy-dependent fertilization processes.


Other Options:
Option A: The tall includes the middle piece, principal piece, and end plece but is a general term; mitochondria are specifically in the middle piece.
Option C: Principal piece contains fibrous sheaths for structural support, but no mitochondria.
Option D: The connecting piece links the sperm head to the tall but does not contain mitochondria.

stem: Choose the correct options from the given statements about the enteric nervous system: 1. Vagus is the only afferent from the gut 2. Functions independent of the Brain and Spinal Cord 3. Myenteric plexus in the lamina propria 4. Exclusively under the influence of nerves 5. Only parasympathetic connects the enteric to the CNS
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The enteric nervous system (ENS) functions independently of the brain and spinal cord, earning the nickname "little brain" of the gut.
• It contains about 100 million neurons, organized into the myenteric (Auerbach) plexus and submucosal (Meissner) plexus.
• ENS controls Gl motility, secretion, and blood flow autonomously, but communicates with the CNS via parasympathetic and sympathetic fibers.
• Parasympathetic Input (vagus and pelvic nerves) generally stimulates motility and secretion, releasing acetylcholine or substance P. Sympathetic Input inhibits motility and stimulates sphincters via cellac, mesenteric, and hypogastric ganglia.


Other Options:
Option A: Incorrect because myenteric plexus is between circular and longitudinal muscle layers, not in lamina propria, and vagus is not the only afferent.
Option B: Incorrect: ENS is not exclusively under neural control; It also responds to local chemical and mechanical stimuli.
Option C: Incorrect; vagus is not the only afferent, and myenteric plexus is not in lamina propria.

stem: What causes a decrease in GFR?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) depends on the glomerular filtration coefficient (Kf), net filtration pressure, and surface area of the glomerular capillaries.
• Kf is determined by: 1. Surface area available for filtration: reduced in glomerular damage or disease. 2. Permeability of the glomerular membrane: decreased in conditions like diabetic nephropathy.
• A decrease In Kf reduces the efficiency of the glomerulus to filter plasma, leading to a lower GFR.
• Other determinants of GFR: 1. Pc (glomerular hydrostatic pressure): favors filtration; increase raises GFR. 2. Ng (glomerular oncotic pressure): opposes filtration; decrease increases GFR. 3. Pb (Bowman's capsule hydrostatic pressure): opposes filtration; decrease increases GFR.
DeterminantsPhysiological/Pathophysiological Causes
Kf (filtration coefficient) -> GFRRenal disease, diabetes mellitus, hypertension
PB -> GFRUrinary tract obstruction (e.g., kidney stones)
TG -> GFRRenal blood flow increased plasma proteins
PG -> GFR
a) afferent arteriolar resistance (RA) -> PGSympathetic activity, (e.g., norepinephrine, endothelin)
b) systemic arterial pressure(Ap) -> PGArterial pressure
c) efferent arteriolar resistance (RE) -> PGAngiotensin II (drugs that block angiotensin II formation)


Other Options:
Option B: Incorrect because a decrease in glomerular oncotic pressure actually Increases GFR.
Option C: Incorrect; an increase in glomerular hydrostatic pressure Increases GFR, not decreases.
Option D: Incorrect; a decrease in Bowman's capsule hydrostatic pressure favors filtration and increases GFR.

stem: A 70-year-old man has a BP of 170/100 mmHg. What is the reason for the Increased pulse pressure in this patient?
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Pulse pressure (PP) = Systolic BP-Diastolic BP; it reflects stroke volume and arterial compliance.
• Decreased arterial compliance (stiffer arteries due to age or arteriosclerosis) leads to higher systolic BP and lower diastolic BP, widening the pulse pressure.
• PP Is directly proportional to stroke volume and Inversely proportional to arterial compliance: PP ~ Stroke volume / Arterial compliance.
• Widened pulse pressure (>40 mmHg) is common in elderly patients due to arterial stiffening.
• Other causes of widened pulse pressure include aortic regurgitation, aortic sclerosis, severe anemia, and hyperthyroidism.
Type of Blood PressureDeterminant
Systolic BP (SBP)Stroke volume (directly proportional)
Compliance of aorta (inversely proportional)
Diastolic BP (DBP)Elastic recoil of aorta/major vessels (directly proportional)
Pulse pressure (PP)Ratio of stroke volume to compliance of the arterial tree
Mean blood pressure (MAP)Cardiac output
Total peripheral resistance


Other Options:
Option B: Incorrect: increased cardiac output alone does not significantly increase pulse pressure in older adults with stiff arteries.
Option C: Incorrect; sympathetic outflow increases heart rate and contractility but does not primarily cause widened pulse pressure.
Option D: Incorrect; increased myocardial contractility elevates systolic BP but the main driver of widened pulse pressure in elderly is arterial stiffness.

stem: Physiological dead space:
options: Is lesser than anatomical dead space
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Physiological dead space is the portion of the tidal volume that does not participate in gas exchange.
• It includes anatomic dead space (air in conducting airways) and alveolar dead space (alveoli that are ventilated but not perfused).
• In healthy Individuals, alveolar dead space is negligible, so physiological dead space = anatomical dead space.
• Represents alveoll that receive ventilation but Inadequate perfusion, leading to wasted ventilation.
• Cannot be measured directly by spirometry; requires analysis such as the Bohr equation or single-breath N2 technique.


Other Options:
Option A: Incorrect spirometry measures volumes and flows but cannot determine dead space.
Option B: Incorrect: physiological dead space is usually equal to or greater than anatomic dead space.
Option C: Incorrect; it does not represent inadequately ventilated alveoli (that would reduce ventilation).

stem: Peripheral chemoreceptors are most sensitive to?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Peripheral chemoreceptors are located in the carotid bodies (at the bifurcation of the common carotid arteries) and aortic bodies (near the aortic arch).
• They are highly sensitive to drops in arterial oxygen (PaO2 <60 mmHg), making hypoxia the primary stimulus.
• Mechanism: Hypoxia inactivates O2-sensitive K channels in Type I glomus cells -> reduced K efflux -> depolarization -> Ca influx -> action potentials -> stimulation of afferent nerves to medullary respiratory centers -> increased respiratory rate and depth.
• Peripheral chemoreceptors also respond to hypercapnia and acidosis, but central chemoreceptors are the main detectors for CO2 and pH changes.
• Response to alkalosis is weak and less physiologically significant compared to hypoxia.


Other Options:
Option A: Incorrect; hypercapnia primarily stimulates central chemoreceptors rather than peripheral ones.
Option C: Incorrect; acidosis has a modest effect on peripheral chemoreceptors, central chemoreceptors dominate pH sensing.
Option D: Incorrect; alkalosis produces only minimal activation of peripheral chemoreceptors.
