Expired/discarded medicine, cytotoxic drugsIncineration/plasma pyrolysis/disposal in a secured landfill. Disposal- Incineration/plasma pyrolysis Encapsulation- hazardous waste treatment, storage, and disposal facility.
Chemical waste / Chemical liquid wasteAfter resource recovery, the chemical liquid waste shall be pre-treated before mixing with other wastewater.

Type of Waste (Red Bin)Treatment and Disposal Options
Recyclable contaminated waste (tubing, bottles, IV tubes, catheters, syringes without needles, gloves)Autoclaving/Microwaving/Hydroclaving Followed by Shredding/mutilation And later, Energy recovery/Plastics to diesel or fuel oil/road-making. Not to be sent to landfill.

Type of Waste (White Translucent)Treatment and Disposal Options
Waste Sharps including metals (needles, syringes with fixed needles, scalpels, blades)Autoclaving/Dry heat sterilization Followed by- Shredding/mutilation/ encapsulation Later by - Iron foundries/sanitary landfill/waste sharps.

Type of Waste (Blue)Treatment and Disposal Options
Glassware (broken/discarded glass, medicine vials/ampules), Metallic body implantsSodium hypochlorite/Autoclaving/Microwaving/Hydroclaving Later-Recycling.



Other Options:
Option A: Red bin is for contaminated recyclable waste like tubing and plastic Items, usually for autoclaving and recycling.
Option C: Blue bin is for glassware and metallic body implants.
Option D: Black bin is for general non-Infectious waste.

stem: Universal health coverage (UHC) means that all people and communities receive the quality health services they need, without financial hardship. Which of the following are the challenges to implementing UHC?
options: Inequalities in the distribution, adequate support, and access to quality-assured products only in low-income settings
The purpose of UHC is to find a balance between countries' Income, health provision, and financial burden
To provide healthcare to everyone regardless of the country's Income
UHC only deals with the financial aspect of health and cannot cover health provisions for all
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• A major challenge in Implementing UHC is providing healthcare to everyone regardless of the country's Income level.
• Low- and middle-income countries often face difficulty in mobilizing sufficient resources to ensure equitable access to quality health services.
• UHC requires balancing avallability, accessibility, and affordability of healthcare services across all populations.
• Ensuring quality healthcare without financial hardship for all populations is complex and resource-Intensive, especially in resource-limited settings.


Other Options:
Option A: Highlights inequities in access, but the challenge is broader than only low-income settings.
Option B: Describes the purpose of UHC rather than a specific challenge.
Option D: Incorrectly limits UHC to financial aspects; UHC also encompasses access to comprehensive health services.

stem: Which is not a part of the Anemia Mukt Bharat Programme?
subject: PSM, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The Anemla Mukt Bharat (AMB) Programme follows the 6x6x6 strategy:
1. 6 beneficiarles: Children (6-59 months), school-age children (6-10 years), adolescents (10-19 years), women of reproductive age, pregnant women, and lactating mothers.
2. 6 Interventions: IFA supplementation, dietary diversification, deworming, intensified behavior change communication, treatment of hemoglobinopathies, and use of fortified foods.
3. 6 Institutional mechanisms: Governance structures at national, state, district, block, facility, and community levels to implement the program.
• 6 health functionaries are not part of this official framework.


Other Options:
Option A: 6 beneficiarles are included in the program's strategy.
Option B: 6 Interventions are key components of AMB.
Option C: 6 institutional mechanisms ensure program implementation at different administrative levels.

stem: Early onset pre-eclampsia is defined before:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Early-onset pre-eclampsia is defined as pre-eclampsia developing before 34 weeks of gestation.
• It is commonly associated with abnormal placentation, leading to placental insufficiency.
• Early-onset disease has a higher risk of severe maternal complications, including eclampsia, HELLP syndrome, and acute renal failure.
• Fetal complications such as fetal growth restriction, oligohydramnios, and preterm birth are more frequent.
• Management often requires close surveillance and early delivery if maternal or fetal status deteriorates.
• Features of pre-eclampsia: 1. Hypertension: Blood pressure >=140/90 mmHg on 2 occasions at least 4 hours apart, or >=160/110 mmHg in a single measurement. 2. Proteinurla: 300 mg of protein in a 24-hour urine collection, Protein/creatinine ratio 0.3. Dipstick reading of >=1+ (though less specific). 3. End-Organ Dysfunction (may occur even without proteinuria): Renal: Elevated serum creatinine >1.1 mg/dL or doubling of baseline. 4. Hepatic: Elevated liver transaminases (AST/ALT) Hematologic: Thrombocytopenia (<100,000/microL). 5. Neurologic: Severe headache, visual disturbances (e.g., scotoma, blurred vision). 6. Pulmonary: Pulmonary edema.


Other Options:
Option A: 28 weeks is earlier than the standard cutoff used to define early-onset pre-eclampsia.
Option B: 30 weeks is not used as a diagnostic threshold in standard obstetric definitions.
Option C: 32 weeks is still earlier than the accepted cutoff of 34 weeks.

stem: Dr Priya is conducting a talk on the screening of cervical cancer with a Pap smear. She works in a clinic in a low-resource setting and she was planning to screen a woman in her 20s. Which of the following is true regarding cervical cancer screening guidelines in her workplace?
options: Pap smear every 3 years, starting at 21 years
Co-testing (Pap and hrHPV) every 5 years starting at 25 years
hrHPV testing every 5 years starting at 30 years
Pap smear every 5 years starting at 21 years
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• In low-resource settings, cytology-based screening (Pap smear) remains the recommended and feasible method.
• Cervical cancer screening is advised to start at 21 years, irrespective of sexual activity.
• For women aged 21-29 years, Pap smear alone every 3 years is the standard recommendation.
• HPV testing is not routinely recommended in women in their 20s due to high rates of transient HPV infections.
• Pap smear allows early detection of precancerous lesions (CIN), enabling timely intervention.
• Pap Smear Schedule:
• 1. Time for Initiation: 21 years, regardless of the age of first sexual intercourse.
• 2. Women aged 21-29 years: Every 3 years (earlier it was done annually).
• 3. Women aged 30-65 years: Pap test + HPV testing every 5 years.
• 4. Women should stop having cervical cancer screening after the age of 65 years. If suspicious PAP, screening till 75 years.
• 5. HIV+ women: annual checkup until three consecutive negative tests followed by testing every 3 years for life.
• 6. HPV DNA testing in combination with PAP improves the sensitivity of screening.
• Ayres spatula and endocervical brush are the instruments used for the Pap test.
• Ayres spatula: One end is blunt, and the other end is bifid. The blunt end is used to take samples for vaginal cytology. Bifid end is used to take a Pap smear.


Other Options:
Option B: Co-testing is recommended in higher-resource settings and usually starts at an older age group.
Option C: Primary hrHPV testing is recommended from 30 years onward, not in women in their 20s.
Option D: A 5-year interval is not recommended for Pap smear alone.

stem: 26-year P1L1 presents with abdominal pain. She has a past history of rheumatic heart disease for which she has undergone mitral valve replacement surgery and is on oral tablet warfarin 3 mg OD. On examination, an abdominal mass corresponding to 14 weeks was felt. On USG, an 8x6 cm unilocular cyst with a reticular pattern was observed in the left ovary. What is the next line of management?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• A unilocular ovarian cyst with a reticular (fish-net/lacy) pattern on ultrasound is characteristic of a hemorrhagic functional ovarian cyst.
• Functional ovarian cysts in reproductive-age women are usually benign and self-limiting, with a high likelihood of spontaneous resolution.
• Conservative management with repeat ultrasonography after 6-12 weeks is the recommended approach to document resolution.
• The patient is on long-term anticoagulation (warfarin) following valve replacement, which significantly increases perioperative bleeding risk.
• Immediate surgical intervention is avoided unless there are complications such as torsion, rupture, or suspicion of malignancy.
SizeManagement
< 5 cmWait & watch; no additional treatment required
5 cm-7 cmFollow up with serial TVS in 6-12 weeks; if cyst persists continue TVS on yearly basis.
> 7 cmMRI pelvis; surgical evaluation



Other Options:
Option A: CA-125 estimation is not indicated in young women with ultrasound features suggestive of a benign functional cyst.
Option C: Surgery is reserved for persistent, enlarging, or complicated cysts and is not first-line in this scenario.
Option D: MRI pelvis is not routinely required when ultrasound findings are typical of a hemorrhagic cyst.

stem: When is delayed cord clamping done?
options: Term and preterm bables irrespective of birth weights
Vigorous term and preterm bables requiring resuscitation
All term babies born at 37 weeks
subject: Pediatrics, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Delayed cord clamping (DCC) is defined as clamping the umbilical cord 30-60 seconds after birth.
• It is recommended for both term and preterm neonates, irrespective of birth weight, provided the baby does not require immediate resuscitation.
• DCC improves neonatal blood volume, increases iron stores, and reduces the risk of iron deficiency anemia in infancy.
• In preterm infants, DCC reduces the incidence of intraventricular hemorrhage, necrotizing enterocolitis, and need for blood transfusion.
• Current obstetric guidelines recommend DCC as a part of routine delivery care.


Other Options:
Option B: Babies requiring immediate resuscitation usually need early cord clamping.
Option C: Requirement of resuscitation is a contraindication to delayed cord clamping.
Option D: DCC is not limited only to term babies born at 37 weeks.

stem: A couple presents with infertility even after 2 years of unprotected intercourse. The seminal analysis reveals a volume of 2 mL, concentration of 40 million/mL, progressive motility of 55%, and morphology of 45%. The wife's hormone levels were normal with bilateral spillage of dye from both fallopian tubes on HSG. What percentage of couples can be attributed to unexplained infertility?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Unexplained infertility is diagnosed when standard evaluation shows normal ovulation, normal tubal patency, and normal semen parameters, yet conception does not occur.
• In the given scenario, the male partner has normal semen volume, count, motility, and morphology, and the female partner has normal hormonal profile with bilateral tubal patency, fulfilling criteria for unexplained infertility.
• Epidemiological data indicate that approximately 20-25% of infertile couples fall into this category.
• Possible underlying factors include subtle ovulatory dysfunction, sperm-oocyte interaction defects, implantation failure, or immunological factors, which are not detected on routine testing.
• This diagnosis is one of exclusion, made only after thorough evaluation of both partners.
• Some causes of unexplained infertility include:
• 1. Ovulatory Dysfunction
• 2. Impaired sperm function despite normal semen analysis
• 3. Tubal or Uterine Factors
• 4. Endometriosis
• 5. Antisperm antibodies or autoimmune issues.
• 6. Lifestyle and Environmental Factors: Stress, smoking, diet, or environmental toxins.


Other Options:
Option A: 5% significantly underestimates the prevalence of unexplained infertility.
Option B: 30% is higher than the commonly quoted range in standard textbooks.
Option C: 15% represents the lower end of estimates but is less commonly cited than 20-25%.

stem: Which of the following is not a physiological change seen in pregnancy?
options: Increase in systolic and diastolic blood pressure after 6 weeks
Peripheral resistance to insulin leading to hyperinsulinemia
Increased plasma volume and RBC mass
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• In normal pregnancy, there is a decrease in systemic vascular resistance due to progesterone-mediated vasodilatation.
• This results in a physiological fall in both systolic and diastolic blood pressure, beginning early in pregnancy and reaching a nadir in the second trimester.
• Blood pressure gradually returns to pre-pregnancy levels only in the third trimester.
• An increase in blood pressure after 6 weeks of gestation is not physiological and suggests an underlying pathology such as chronic hypertension.
• Therefore, an early rise in blood pressure is considered abnormal in pregnancy.
IncreasesDecreases
Hemoglobin mass, Total plasma protein, Globulin, Neutrophils, Fibrinogen, Factors 7,8,9,10, Insulin, LDL, HDL, apolipoproteinsHemoglobin concentration, S.urea/creatinine/uric acid, Albumin, Platelets, Blood urea nitrogen, Factor 11,13, S.Na/K/Ca/Mg/12
S.Transferrin and TIBC (Total Iron binding capacity)S.Iron, ferritin
CRP (C-Reactive protein)
Placental phosphatase
Total alkaline phosphatase
ESR 4x rise



Other Options:
Option B: Pregnancy induces insulin resistance due to placental hormones, leading to compensatory hyperinsulinemia.
Option C: Increased fetal glucose utilization causes fasting hypoglycemia, while insulin resistance causes postprandial hyperglycemia.
Option D: Plasma volume increases by about 40-50% and RBC mass also increases, producing physiological hemodilution.

stem: Instrument assisted delivery is not indicated in which of the following?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Cephalopelvic disproportion (CPD) implies a mismatch between the fetal head size and the maternal pelvis.
• In CPD, vaginal delivery is not mechanically possible, even with assistance.
• Instrument-assisted delivery (forceps or vacuum) requires that CPD must be excluded before application.
• Attempting instrumental delivery in CPD increases the risk of maternal soft tissue injury, uterine rupture, and fetal trauma.
• The definitive and safest mode of delivery in true CPD is cesarean section.
ProcedureCriteria
Outlet forcepsScalp is visible at the introitus without separating the labia. Fetal skull has reached pelvic floor. Fetal head is at or on perineum. Sagittal suture is in anteroposterior diameter or right or left occiput anterior or posterior position. Rotation does not exceed 45 degrees.
Low forcepsLeading point of fetal skull is at station >=+2 cm and not on the pelvic floor. 1. a) Rotation is 45 degrees or less, or 2. b) Rotation is greater than 45 degrees.
Mid forcepsThe fetal head is between 0 and +2 cm.
High forcepsNot included in classification. Not used anymore.



Other Options:
Option A: Instrumental delivery can be used to expedite delivery in fetal distress when vaginal delivery is imminent.
Option B: Maternal exhaustion is a common indication for assisted vaginal delivery.
Option C: Prolonged second stage of labour is an accepted indication for instrumental delivery if prerequisites are fulfilled.

stem: In which stage of meiosis is the primary oocyte arrested?
subject: Anatomy, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Primary oocytes are arrested in prophase I of meiosis, specifically in the diplotene stage.
• This arrest begins during intrauterine life, and the oocytes remain suspended at this stage until puberty.
• The prolonged arrest phase is also called the dictyotene stage, characterized by dispersed chromosomes.
• With the onset of puberty, under the influence of FSH and LH, a selected oocyte resumes meiosis during each menstrual cycle.
• Completion of meiosis I occurs just before ovulation, forming a secondary oocyte and first polar body.


Other Options:
Option B: Leptotene is an early phase of prophase I where chromosomes begin to condense.
Option C: Zygotene is the stage where homologous chromosomes start pairing (synapsis).
Option D: Pachytene is the stage where crossing over and genetic recombination occur.

stem: Restitution is best described as:
options: Passive movement of head due to untwisting of neck
Movement of the head by 1/8th of the circle due to torsion
Stretching of the vulval outlet by the head
External rotation after delivery of shoulder
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Restitution refers to the passive movement of the fetal head after its delivery to correct the torsion produced during internal rotation.
• During descent and internal rotation, the neck becomes twisted as the shoulders remain in the oblique diameter while the head rotates to the anteroposterior diameter.
• After the head is delivered, it realigns with the shoulders, resulting in a slight rotation (about one-eighth of a circle).
• This movement is passive, driven by elastic recoil of the neck and not by uterine contractions.
• Restitution precedes external rotation, which occurs with the delivery of the shoulders.


Other Options:
Option B: Describes the degree of movement but not the mechanism of restitution.
Option C: Refers to distension of the vulval outlet during crowning, not restitution.
Option D: Describes external rotation, which occurs after restitution.

stem: Which of the following is NOT part of AMTSL?
options: Delivery of placenta by controlled cord traction
Administration of oxytocin after shoulder delivery
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Active Management of the Third Stage of Labour (AMTSL) is recommended to prevent postpartum haemorrhage (PPH).
• The core components of AMTSL include:
• 1. Administration of a uterotonic (oxytocin) soon after delivery of the baby (after delivery of the anterior shoulder or within 1 minute of birth)
• 2. Controlled cord traction (CCT) for delivery of the placenta
• 3. Uterine massage after delivery of the placenta to maintain uterine tone
• Bimanual uterine compression is not a routine component of AMTSL. It is a therapeutic measure used in the management of established PPH, particularly due to uterine atony, when initial measures fail.
Step NumberProcedureDetails
1.Injecting uterotonics within 1 minute of delivery of the baby or immediately after delivery of the anterior shoulder.Oxytocin 10 IU im/iv infusion is the preferred uterotonic. (Not recommended by WHO- carboprost)
2.Delayed cord clampingDone within 1-3 mins of delivery. 80 ml of additional blood goes to the fetus. Prevents fetal anemia.
3.Delivery of placenta by controlled cord traction: Modified Brandt Andrews techniqueThe uterus is pushed upwards and backward. The traction of cord is downwards and forwards.
4.Intermittent assessment of uterine toneDone every 15 mins for 2 hours.



Other Options:
Option A: Uterine massage is a component of AMTSL after placental delivery.
Option B: Controlled cord traction is an essential step in AMTSL.
Option D: Administration of oxytocin after shoulder delivery is the most important component of AMTSL.

stem: A 35-year-old P2L2 woman presents with malodorous vaginal discharge and a normal cervix. The whiff test is positive. What is this condition?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Bacterial vaginosis (BV) results from an imbalance in the vaginal microbiome, with overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria such as Gardnerella vaginalis and a reduction in protective lactobacilli.
• BV is characterized by malodorous, fishy-smelling vaginal discharge and usually a normal-appearing cervix.
• The positive whiff test (amine test) supports the diagnosis, caused by the release of volatile amines when KOH is added to vaginal secretions.
• Common causative organisms include Gardnerella, Mobiluncus, and Mycoplasma hominis.
• BV is typically not associated with significant Inflammation or systemic symptoms.
• Under microscopy, many epithelial cells present with granular cytoplasm caused by small gram-negative bacilli adhering on their surface, these cells are called clue cells.
• Treatment is oral metronidazole 500 mg BD for 7 days. Alternatively, Ampicillin 500 mg or cephalosporin 500 mg bid for 7 days is also effective.
FeatureBacterial vaginosisTrichomonas vaginitisCandidal vaginitis
Causative organismAltered vaginal flora- increased concentration of Gardnerella vaginalisTrichomonas vaginalisCandida albicans
AetiologyIncreased vaginal pHSexually transmittedDecreased immunity
Sexual transmissionNoYesNo
DischargeFishy, thin, whitish to grayFrothy, greenCurdy, white
SymptomsFishy odour, no itchingItching and burningItching, burning
pH>4.5>4.5<4.5
SpeculumNo vaginal epithelium inflammationStrawberry cervixInflamed-epithelium
KOHPositive Whiff testThe whiff test can be positive.Pseudohyphae seen.
Wet mountClue cells. No WBCs seenMotile trichomonads, WBCS seenWBCs seen
TreatmentMetronidazole or ClindamycinMetronidazole, treat partner alsoOral or vaginal azole, no need to treat a partner.



Other Options:
Option A: Candida infection usually presents with thick, curdy, white discharge and pruritus, not a positive whiff test.
Option B: Chlamydia may be asymptomatic or cause mucoid discharge, but the whiff test is not positive.
Option D: Pelvic inflammatory disease involves upper genital tract infection, often with cervical motion tenderness, uterine or adnexal tenderness, and systemic symptoms.

stem: A 14-year-old came with complaints of acute urinary retention, She has not attained menarche and has cyclic abdominal pain. On genital examination, a bluish bulge is seen. What is the probable diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Imperforate hymen is a congenital obstruction of the vaginal outlet causing retention of menstrual blood (hematocolpos) after menarche.
• Patients present with primary amenorrhea, cyclical lower abdominal pain, and sometimes acute urinary retention due to compression of the urethra.
• On examination, a bluish bulge at the vaginal introitus is typical, representing distended vaginal mucosa filled with blood.
• Ultrasound may show hematocolpos or hematometra depending on severity. Treatment involves hymenotomy or cruciate incision to relieve obstruction and allow normal menstrual flow.


Disorders of Sexual Development:
DisorderCauseReproductive organsBreastAxillary/Pubic hairKaryotype
Complete androgen InsensitivityX-linked mutation of androgen receptorAbsent uterus and upper vagina, cryptorchid testisYesMinimal to absent46 XY
Mullerian agenesisHypoplastic or absent Mullerian ductal system Also called Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndromeAbsent or rudimentary uterus and upper vagina, normal ovariesYesNormal46 XX
Turner syndromeComplete or partial absence of one X chromosomeNormal uterus and vagina, streak gonadsVariableNormal45 XO or 45 X mosaicism
Complete XY dysgenesis (Swyer syndrome)SRY gene mutation on the Y chromosomeNormal uterus and vagina, streak gonadsNoMinimal to absent46 XY

Other Options:
Option A: Transverse vaginal septum can cause hematocolpos but typically presents as a high vaginal mass, not a bluish bulge at the introitus.
Option C: MRKH presents with primary amenorrhea but absent uterus/vagina, so there is no cyclical pain or hematocolpos.
Option D: OHVIRA (Obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal anomaly) presents with pelvic pain but usually after menarche and is associated with renal agenesis.

stem: Which of the following does not constitute a germ cell tumor?
subject: Pathology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Juvenile granulosa cell tumor (JGCT) is a sex cord-stromal tumor, not a germ cell tumor.
• Germ cell tumors arise from primordial germ cells of the ovary and include dysgerminoma, yolk sac tumor, and non-gestational choriocarcinoma.
• JGCT usually occurs in children and adolescents and may present with precocious puberty due to estrogen secretion.
• These tumors are typically solid, unilateral, and have good prognosis if detected early.
• Management involves surgical excision, often unilateral oophorectomy, depending on stage and fertility considerations.
• Germ cell tumors:
1. Dysgerminoma
2. Endodermal sinus tumor (Yolk sac tumor)
3. Embryonal carcinoma
4. Polyembryoma
5. Non-gestational choriocarcinoma
6. Teratoma (Mature/immature)
7. Mixed forms

WHO Classification of Ovarian Tumors:
I. Epithelial tumors (most common)
• Serous tumors
• Mucinous tumors
• Endometrioid tumors
• Clear cell
• Brenner tumors (transitional cell tumors)
• Seromucinous
• Undifferentiated carcinoma
II. Sex cord-stromal tumors:
• Pure stromal cell tumors - Fibroma, Thecoma, Leydig cell tumor, Steroid cell tumor
• Pure sex cord tumors - Granulosa cell tumor (Adult/Juvenile), Sertoli cell tumor
• Mixed sex cord-stromal tumors - Sertoli-Leydig cell tumors
III. Germ cell tumors:
• Dysgerminoma
• Endodermal sinus tumor (Yolk sac tumor)
• Embryonal carcinoma
• Polyembryoma
• Non-gestational choriocarcinoma
• Teratoma (Mature/immature)
• Mixed forms
IV. Gonadoblastoma
• Pure
• Mixed with dysgerminoma or other germ cell tumors
V. Soft tissue tumors not specific to ovary
VI. Miscellaneous tumors
VII. Secondary (metastatic) tumors
VIII. Tumor-like conditions


Other Options:
Option A: Yolk sac tumor is a malignant germ cell tumor producing AFP.
Option B: Dysgerminoma is the ovarian counterpart of seminoma and is a germ cell tumor.
Option D: Non-gestational choriocarcinoma is a rare germ cell tumor producing hCG.

stem: Which of the following is the smallest pelvic diameter at the brim?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The obstetric conjugate is the smallest fixed diameter of the pelvic inlet through which the fetal head must pass.
• It is measured from the midpoint of the sacral promontory to the closest point on the inner surface of the pubic symphysis.
• Its average length is approximately 10 cm, representing the narrowest obstetric diameter.
• It cannot be measured directly during a pelvic exam and is estimated from the diagonal conjugate.
• This diameter is crucial in assessing the adequacy of the pelvis for vaginal delivery.


Other Options:
Option B: Diagonal conjugate is measured clinically from the sacral promontory to the inferior margin of the pubic symphysis and is longer than the obstetric conjugate.
Option C: True conjugate is another term for the obstetric conjugate but sometimes used interchangeably; the smallest obstetric measurement is still the obstetric conjugate.
Option D: Anatomical conjugate is measured from the sacral promontory to the upper margin of the pubic symphysis, slightly longer than the obstetric conjugate.

stem:
A female with abdominal pain and bleeding per vagina with 3 months of amenorrhea. On examination her BP was 90/60 mmHg. Which of the following statements is/are true about ectopic pregnancy?
1. Abdominal pain is the most common presenting symptom of ectopic pregnancy and may be present in at least 90% of the patients.
2. PID and sexually transmitted infections are a major risk factor.
3. Low values of progesterone are usually diagnostic of ectopic pregnancy, however there's no specific value to completely rule out or exclude ectopic pregnancy.
4. Oral methotrexate is a viable medical alternative to surgery in ectopic pregnancy patients presenting with shock or renal failure.
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Abdominal pain is the most common presenting symptom of ectopic pregnancy, occurring in at least 90% of patients.
• Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) and sexually transmitted infections are significant risk factors for ectopic pregnancy due to tubal damage.
• Low serum progesterone can suggest ectopic pregnancy, but there is no absolute cutoff value to rule it out completely.
• Medical management with oral methotrexate is contraindicated in patients presenting with shock, hemodynamic instability, or renal/hepatic failure; such patients require emergency surgical intervention.

Risk factors of ectopic pregnancy:
• Previous ectopic pregnancy
• Tubal corrective surgery, tubectomy
• Assisted reproductive technology
• Documented tubal pathology
• Pelvic inflammatory disease (Chlamydial infection), genital tuberculosis, sexually transmitted diseases
• Endometriosis, Pelvic adhesions
• Prior abortion, Prior Caesarean delivery
• Multiple sexual partners
• Progesterone-only pills (POPs)

Management of ectopic pregnancy:
1. Medical management, using intramuscular methotrexate (statement 4), is preferred in the following cases:
• Low initial serum beta-hCG level (Less than 5,000 IU/L)
• Small ectopic pregnancy size (Less than 3.5 cm)
• Absent fetal cardiac activity
• Hemodynamically stable

2. Expectant management: A low initial serum hCG concentration, a considerable reduction in levels over 48 hours, and a sonographic inhomogeneous mass instead of a tubal halo or other gestational features are predictive variables for success.
3. Surgical management with salpingectomy or salpingostomy is done when:
• Gestational sac >3.5 cm
• Fetal cardiac activity present
• Beta-hCG >5000 mIU/mL
• Significant pain and hemoperitoneum

Radiological Signs in Obstetrics:
Bagel Sign (USG) It is seen in unruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy. It is also known as the tubal ring sign or the blob sign. It refers to the appearance of an anechoic gestational sac in the adnexa surrounded by a hyperechoic ring.
Twin Peak Sign/ Lambda Sign (USG):
Chorionicity of the placenta is best diagnosed by ultrasound at 10-13 weeks of gestation. In dichorionic twins, there is a thick triangular projection of placental tissue between the two gestational sacs. This is known as the lambda sign or twin peak sign.
T Sign (USG):
The T sign which is seen in monochorionic diamniotic gestation is thin and T-shaped because there is no extension of placental tissue or chorion in between the amniotic membranes.
Double Decidual Sac Sign (USG):
It is seen in early intrauterine pregnancy as two concentric echogenic rings surrounding the gestational sac.
Lemon Sign (USG):
At the level of the lateral ventricles, there is inward bowing or scalloping of frontal bones giving rise to the lemon sign. It is suggestive of spina bifida. It is also characteristically associated with Chiari II Malformation.
Banana Sign (USG):
Anterior curvature of the cerebellum with effacement of the cisterna magna in the posterior fossa is seen as a banana sign.
Spalding sign (USG):
It usually appears 7 days after intrauterine death and is caused by liquefaction of the brain matter and softening of the ligamentous structures supporting the vault, leading to irregular overlapping of the cranial bones on one another.
Frog Eye Sign (USG):
It is seen in anencephaly. Due to the absence of calvaria, the orbits appear more prominent. This is known as the "frog-eye sign".
Omphalocele (USG):
It is a congenital midline abdominal wall defect at the base of the umbilical cord insertion, with herniation of the solid abdominal viscera and/or bowel out of the fetal abdomen (indicated with an asterisk). The extruded abdominal contents are covered in a sac.
Double Bubble Sign (USG):
Dilatation of stomach and first part of duodenum due to duodenal atresia.
Bunch of grapes sign or Snowstorm appearance (USG):
The appearance of multiple cystic spaces or lesions in a complete mole.
Ball sign (X-ray)
It is due to the crumpled up spine of the fetus seen in case of intrauterine death.
Robert's sign (X-ray)
The presence of gas in the fetal great vessels in case of intrauterine fetal demise is known as Robert's sign.
Stuck twin sign (USG)
The smaller twin is held in a fixed position along the uterine wall due to the severe oligohydramnios. It is seen in twin-twin transfusion syndrome.
Butterfly sign (USG)
Normal appearance of the choroid plexuses on axial imaging of the fetal brain. An absence of this sign suggests holoprosencephaly.
Cloverleaf skull (USG)
Seen in craniosynostosis
Empty amnion sign (USG)
Visualization of an amniotic sac without concomitant visualization of an embryo. It is an indicator of pregnancy failure.


Other Options:
Option A: Includes only statements 1 and 2, missing statement 3 which is also correct.
Option B: Includes statements 3 and 4; statement 4 is incorrect in unstable patients.
Option D: Includes statement 4, which is incorrect in patients with shock or organ failure.

stem: A 32-year-old woman, G2P1L1, comes for a quadruple test at 16 weeks. Which of the following is not included in the quadruple test?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Dimeric Inhibin B is not part of the standard quadruple test; the test uses dimeric Inhibin A instead.
• The quadruple test is a second-trimester maternal serum screening for chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., Down syndrome, Edwards syndrome) and neural tube defects.
• The four markers measured are: alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), unconjugated estriol (uE3), beta hCG, and dimeric inhibin A.
• Elevated or decreased levels of these markers help in risk assessment, not definitive diagnosis.
• The test is typically performed between 15-20 weeks of gestation, optimally around 16 weeks.


The following table summarizes the findings of the quad-screening test in various conditions:
ParameterTrisomy 21Trisomy 18Neural tube/ abdominal wall defect
MSAFPLowLowHigh
BHCGHighLowNormal
Unconjugated estriolLowLowNormal
Inhibin AHighNormal/LowNormal

Other Options:
Option B: Unconjugated estriol is a component of the quadruple test.
Option C: AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) is a key marker for neural tube defects and trisomy screening.
Option D: Beta hCG is included to assess risk for trisomy 21.

stem: An athlete with a BMI of 17.5 kg/m2 presented with secondary amenorrhea and primary infertility. Her laboratory investigations reveal FSH 2 mlU/ml; LH 0.32 IU/L; TSH 1.2 mlU/ml; Prolactin 20 ng/ml. What is the underlying cause of this condition?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (HH) is characterized by low FSH and LH levels, leading to secondary amenorrhea and infertility.
• In this patient, low BMI due to excessive exercise or undernutrition is a common cause of functional HH, also termed hypothalamic amenorrhea.
• Other causes of HH include stress, chronic illness, and hypothalamic-pituitary lesions.
• TSH and prolactin are normal, ruling out thyroid dysfunction or hyperprolactinemia.
• Management focuses on nutritional rehabilitation, reducing excessive exercise, and addressing underlying causes to restore normal menstrual function.


Other Options:
Option A: Primary ovarian failure is associated with high FSH and LH due to ovarian insufficiency.
Option C: Hypergonadotrophic hypogonadism is another term for primary ovarian failure, not seen here.
Option D: Normogonadotrophic normogonadism would show normal FSH/LH; this patient has low levels.

stem: Which of the following is not associated with celiac disease?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Celiac disease is strongly associated with HLA-DQ2 and HLA-DQ8, not HLA-DQ6 or HLA-DQ11.
• It is an autoimmune disorder triggered by ingestion of gluten-containing foods such as wheat, barley, rye, and oats (mnemonic: BROW) in genetically predisposed individuals.
• IgA anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies (tTG-IgA) are the preferred diagnostic serologic test for celiac disease.
• IgG antigliadin antibodies are helpful in patients with IgA deficiency.
• The Modified Marsh classification grades intestinal mucosal changes on biopsy, from intraepithelial lymphocytosis to total villous atrophy, and is used to assess disease severity.


Other Options:
Option A: IgA transglutaminase positivity is a key diagnostic marker.
Option B: IgG antigliadin antibodies may be positive, especially in IgA-deficient patients.
Option C: Modified Marsh classification is used to grade histological findings in celiac disease.

stem: All of the following help to prevent the spread of COVID-19 except?
subject: Microbiology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Ivermectin has not been proven to prevent COVID-19 transmission and is not recommended for prophylaxis or treatment of COVID-19 outside clinical trials.
• Large randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews have shown no consistent clinical benefit of ivermectin in preventing infection or reducing disease spread.
• International and national guidelines do not endorse ivermectin for COVID-19 prevention due to lack of efficacy and potential for adverse effects.
• COVID-19 prevention relies primarily on non-pharmacological interventions and vaccination, not unproven antiparasitic drugs.


COVID-19 vaccines approved and in development:

Pfizer/BioNTechmRNA based vaccine
ModernamRNA based vaccine
Oxford University/Astra Zeneca/Serum Institute of India (Covishield)Non-replicating viral vector
Gamaleya (Sputnik V)/ Dr.ReddysNon-replicating viral vector
Johnson and JohnsonNon-replicating viral vector
Bharat biotech/NIV (Covaxin)Inactivated vaccine
Zydus Cadila (ZyCoV-D)Plasmid DNA vaccine
NovavaxProtein subunit

Other Options:
Option A: Cough etiquette reduces droplet spread by covering the mouth and nose during coughing or sneezing.
Option B: Masks reduce transmission by limiting the spread of respiratory droplets and aerosols.
Option D: Vaccination reduces infection rates, disease severity, and community transmission.

stem: Which among the following viruses is primarily water-borne?
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Hepatitis A virus is transmitted primarily by the fecal-oral route, most commonly through contaminated water and food.
• Waterborne outbreaks are common in settings with poor sanitation, unsafe drinking water, and overcrowding.
• The virus is highly stable in the environment and can survive in water for prolonged periods.
• Hepatitis A typically causes acute, self-limited hepatitis and does not progress to chronic liver disease.
• Epidemics are frequently reported following monsoon flooding and sewage contamination of water sources.

Option C: Hepatitis C virus spreads mainly via parenteral exposure such as blood transfusion and needle sharing.
Option D: Hepatitis D virus requires hepatitis B Infection and is transmitted through blood and sexual contact.

stem: A patient presented with a blood pressure of 220/120 mmHg in an altered sensorium. He also had an episode of vomiting and a history of weakness on the left side of his body. Which of the following is Incorrect regarding hypertensive management in this case?
options: In hemorrhagic stroke, reduce the BP to less than 160/100 mmHg with antihypertensives to prevent rebleed
In hemorrhagic stroke, aggressive use of IV antihypertensive if SBP>220
In ER multiple blood pressure readings should be taken before starting anti-hypertensive therapy
In ischemic stroke, BP should be reduced to <140/90 mmHg immediately
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• In acute Ischemic stroke, immediate aggressive blood pressure reduction is avoided to maintain cerebral perfusion, a strategy known as permissive hypertension.
• Blood pressure is not lowered unless SBP>220 mmHg or DBP>120 mmHg, except when thrombolysis is planned.
• For patients eligible for thrombolysis, BP should be lowered to <185/110 mmHg, not to <140/90 mmHg.
• Reducing BP too aggressively in ischemic stroke can worsen cerebral ischemia and increase infarct size.
• Therefore, immediate reduction to <140/90 mmHg is incorrect in the acute ischemic setting.


Other Options:
Option A: Blood pressure control in hemorrhagic stroke helps reduce the risk of hematoma expansion and rebleeding.
Option B: Very high SBP (>220 mmHg) in hemorrhagic stroke warrants IV antihypertensive therapy under close monitoring.
Option C: Multiple BP readings are recommended in the emergency setting to confirm persistent hypertension before treatment.

stem: Which of the following is not a feature of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is characterized by widespread platelet-rich microthrombi due to severe deficiency of ADAMTS13, leading to accumulation of ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers.
• The hallmark features include thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia due to mechanical destruction of red blood cells.
• Neurological manifestations are more prominent in TTP compared to renal involvement.
• Although mild renal dysfunction can occur, severe renal failure is not a defining or predominant feature of TTP.
• Marked renal failure is more characteristic of hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) rather than TTP.


Other Options:
Option A: Thrombocytopenia results from platelet consumption in microvascular thrombi.
Option B: Thrombosis occurs in small vessels due to platelet aggregation.
Option C: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia is caused by red cell fragmentation as they pass through narrowed vessels.

stem: Which of the following cannot help in differentiating psoriatic arthritis from other forms of arthritis?
subject: Medicine, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Psoriatic arthritis (PsA) is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy with characteristic musculoskeletal and extra-articular features.
• Dactylitis (sausage digits) is a classic and distinctive feature of PsA, resulting from combined synovitis and tenosynovitis.
• Enthesitis, inflammation at tendon and ligament insertions, is commonly seen in PsA and helps differentiate it from rheumatoid arthritis.
• Nail pitting, onycholysis, and nail dystrophy are strongly associated with PsA and correlate with distal interphalangeal joint involvement.
• Diarrhea is not a characteristic feature of psoriatic arthritis and does not help in differentiating PsA from other inflammatory arthritides.


Other Options:
Option A: Dactylitis is a hallmark clinical feature of psoriatic arthritis.
Option C: Enthesitis is frequently present in psoriatic arthritis and spondyloarthropathies.
Option D: Nail pitting is a characteristic dermatological association of psoriatic arthritis.

stem: What is the mutation seen in Sickle cell anemia?
subject: Pathology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Sickle cell anemia is caused by a missense point mutation in the B-globin gene on chromosome 11. The mutation involves substitution of glutamic acid by valine at the 6th position of the -globin chain.
• This change replaces a hydrophilic amino acid with a hydrophobic one, creating a sticky hydrophobic patch.
• Under deoxygenated conditions, HbS polymerizes, leading to RBC sickling, hemolysis, and vaso-occlusion. The disease follows an autosomal recessive Inheritance pattern.


Other Options:
Option B: Lysine to arginine substitution is not associated with sickle cell disease.
Option C: Glutamate to lysine substitution does not produce HbS.
Option D: Alanine to glycine substitution is unrelated to hemoglobinopathies.

stem: A female patient with type 1 DM and hypothyroidism presented with chronic oral mucocutaneous candidiasis for 5 years. She is diagnosed with autoimmune polyendocrinopathy syndrome type 1. Which of the following is incorrect about this condition?
subject: Pathology, Medicine, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Autoimmune polyendocrinopathy syndrome type 1 (APS-1), also known as APECED, is a rare autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the AIRE gene.
• The classic triad includes chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and adrenal Insufficiency.
• APS-1 typically begins in childhood or early Infancy, with candidiasis often being the earliest manifestation.
• Type 1 diabetes mellitus is uncommon In APS-1 and is more characteristically associated with APS-2.
• APS type 1 presents in childhood with the following features: 1. Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis 2. Ectodermal dystrophy (abnormalities of the skin, dental enamel, and nails) 3. Organ-specific autoimmune disorders such as: Autoimmune adrenalitis, Autoimmune hypoparathyroidism, Idiopathic hypogonadism, Pernicious anemia.


Other Options:
Option B: APS-1 usually presents in infancy or early childhood.
Option C: Hypoparathyroidism is a core component of APS-1.
Option D: Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis is a hallmark feature of APS-1.

stem: All of the following are examples of HAGMA except?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• High anion gap metabolic acidosis (HAGMA) occurs due to accumulation of unmeasured acids in the blood.
• Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) causes HAGMA due to accumulation of ketoacids.
• Acute kidney Injury (AKI) leads to HAGMA because of reduced excretion of organic acids such as sulfate and phosphate.
• Inborn errors of metabolism cause accumulation of organic acids, resulting in HAGMA.
• Diarrhoea causes loss of bicarbonate from the gastrointestinal tract, leading to normal anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA), not HAGMA.
High Anion-Gap Metabolic Acidosis (MUDPILES)Normal Anion-Gap Metabolic Acidosis (FUSED CAR)
MethanolFistula pancreatic
Uremia (Renal fallure - Acute or Chronic)Ureterosigmoidostomy
Diabetic ketoacidosisSmall bowel fistula
ParaldehydeExtra chloride (Hyperalimentation)
Iron tablets, INHDiarrhea
Lactic acidosisCarbonic anhydrase inhibitor (acetazolamide)
Ethylene glycolAddison's disease
SalicylatesRenal tubular acidosis



Other Options:
Option A: DKA produces excess ketoacids causing high anion gap acidosis.
Option C: AKI results in retention of unmeasured acids leading to HAGMA.
Option D: Inborn metabolic disorders produce organic acids causing HAGMA.

stem: LMN lesions are associated with? 1. Decrease in muscle tone 2. Spastic paralysis 3. Babinski positive 4. Fasciculations
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Lower motor neuron (LMN) lesions result in flaccid paralysis, not spastic paralysis.
• They are characterized by decreased muscle tone (hypotonia) due to loss of excitatory input to the muscle.
• Fasciculations are spontaneous visible twitches of muscle fibers, commonly seen in LMN lesions.
• LMN lesions do not cause spasticity or a positive Babinski sign; these are features of upper motor neuron (UMN) lesions.
SignUpper Motor NeuronLower Motor Neuron
AtrophyNoneSevere
FasciculationsNoneCommon
ToneSpasticDecreased
Distribution of weaknessPyramidal/regionalDistal segmental
Muscle stretch reflexesHyperactiveHypoactive/absent
Babinski signPresentAbsent



Other Options:
Option A: Spastic paralysis is a feature of UMN lesions, not LMN lesions.
Option B: Babinski positivity and spasticity are UMN features.
Option C: Includes both LMN and UMN features and is therefore Incorrect.

stem: In hemolytic anemia 1. Indirect Van den Bergh test is positive 2. Serum conjugated bilirubin is raised. 3. Serum transaminases are raised. 4. Urobilinogen is high 5. GGT is elevated
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The Indirect Van den Bergh test is positive in hemolytic anemia, reflecting elevated unconjugated bilirubin due to increased breakdown of red blood cells.
• Urobilinogen is high because excess bilirubin is converted to urobilinogen in the intestines and reabsorbed, leading to elevated urinary urobilinogen.
• Serum conjugated bilirubin is usually normal or mildly elevated, as the liver can conjugate bilirubin efficiently in most cases.
• Serum transaminases (AST, ALT) and GGT are not typically elevated in hemolytic anemia; they are markers of hepatocellular injury or cholestasis.
• Clinical features of hemolytic anemia include Jaundice, pallor, splenomegaly, and elevated reticulocyte count.
Clinical conditionReaction with reagentInterpretation
Normal serumNo reactionNegative
High conjugated bilirubin eg: Obstructive jaundiceReacts in the absence of methanolDirect positive
High unconjugated bilirubin eg: Hemolytic anaemiaReacts in the presence of methanol to give total bilirubinIndirect positive
High-conjugated and unconjugated bilirubin eg: Hepatic JaundiceReacts strongly both with and without methanolBiphasic reaction



Other Options:
Option A: Includes serum transaminases and GGT, which are not characteristically raised in hemolytic anemia.
Option C: Includes conjugated bilirubin and GGT, which are not primary features of hemolysis.
Option D: Adds GGT, which is unrelated to hemolytic anemia.

stem: A patient has undergone an acute renal allograft transplantation. What would indicate its rejection? (s/o- suggestive of)
options: Any rise in serum creatinine above pre-transplant values is s/o acute renal allograft rejection
Rise of creatinine by more than 5% from the baseline is s/o acute renal allograft rejection
Serum creatinine does not rise in acute renal allograft rejection
Rise of creatinine by more than 10% from the baseline is s/o acute renal allograft rejection
subject: Surgery, Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Acute renal allograft rejection is primarily detected by a rise in serum creatinine above the patient's baseline post-transplant levels.
• A rise of more than 10% from baseline is generally considered suggestive of acute rejection and warrants further evaluation.
• Early recognition is crucial to initiate Immunosuppressive therapy and prevent graft loss.
• Other supportive findings may include oliguria, graft tenderness, fever, and hypertension, but serum creatinine rise is the most sensitive early marker.
• Definitive diagnosis is confirmed by renal allograft biopsy demonstrating cellular or antibody-mediated rejection.
• Acute renal allograft dysfunction is defined as a rise in serum creatinine of >10% from baseline or an absolute rise of 220 µmol/L.
• The causes of early graft dysfunction include: 1. Acute rejection (antibody-mediated or T cell-mediated) 2. Calcineurin inhibitor toxicity 3. Dehydration 4. Urinary tract infection or pyelonephritis 5. Sepsis 6. Renal vein or renal artery thrombosis 7. Ureteric obstruction or urine leak


Other Options:
Option A: Any rise above pre-transplant value is too nonspecific, as minor fluctuations may occur.
Option B: A 5% rise is not clinically significant enough to indicate rejection.
Option C: Serum creatinine rises in acute rejection, so this is incorrect.

stem: A lady presents with recurrent bluing of her hand and stretching of her skin. She also has difficulty in swallowing and lung lesions. What is the pattern of ANA and the diagnosis?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The anti-centromere ANA pattern is strongly associated with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST syndrome).
• Clinical features include Raynaud's phenomenon (recurrent bluing of the hands), sclerodactyly (stretching of the skin), esophageal dysmatility (difficulty swallowing), and pulmonary involvement such as pulmonary fibrosis or hypertension.
• Anti-centromere antibodies are typically seen in limited systemic sclerosis, whereas diffuse systemic sclerosis is more often associated with anti-Sc1-70 (topoisomerase 1) antibodies.
• ANA patterns like speckled, nucleolar, and homogenous may be seen in other connective tissue diseases such as SLE, but the anti-centromere pattern is distinctive for limited systemic sclerosis.
• Early recognition of the antibody pattern helps in prognosis and monitoring organ involvement in systemic sclerosis.
Staining patternAntigen
Homogenous or diffuse nuclear stainingAntibodies to chromatin, histones, and occasionally double-stranded DNA
Rim or peripheral stainingAntibodies to double-stranded DNA, and sometimes to nuclear envelope proteins
Speckled patternOne of the most commonly observed patterns of fluorescence, the least specific Antibodies to non-DNA nuclear constituents such as Sm antigen, ribonucleoprotein, and SS-A and SS-B reactive antigens
Nucleolar patternFew discrete spots of fluorescence within the nucleus and represent antibodies to RNA, Seen systemic sclerosis.
Centromeric patternSeen in systemic sclerosis. Often contain antibodies specific for centromeres, which give rise to this pattern:



Other Options:
Option A: Speckled pattern may be seen in systemic sclerosis but is less specific than anti-centromere.
Option C: Nucleolar pattern is more characteristic of systemic sclerosis (especially diffuse) rather than SLE.
Option D: Homogenous pattern is typically associated with SLE, not systemic sclerosis.

stem: A 60-year-old male patient complains of numbness in both lower limbs. On examination, Romberg test is positive with broad-based gait and loss of proprioception of both lower limbs. He also has a history of low BP, hypothyroidism, and gastrectomy for gastric cancer a year back. Which of the following deficiencies can cause the current condition?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Vitamin B12 deficiency leads to subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord, affecting the dorsal columns (proprioception and vibration) and lateral corticospinal tracts.
• Neurologic signs include numbness, positive Romberg test, broad-based gait, and loss of vibration and position sense.
• Common causes include gastrectomy, pernicious anemia, or malabsorption, which reduce cobalamin absorption.
• Symptoms may occur even without hematologic abnormalities, though macrocytic anemia is often present.
• Early recognition and parenteral B12 replacement can prevent progression of neurologic damage.


Other Options:
Option B: Folic acid deficiency causes macrocytic anemia but does not produce neurologic deficits such as dorsal column dysfunction.
Option C: Thiamine deficiency can cause peripheral neuropathy or Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, but does not affect proprioception or cause spinal cord degeneration.
Option D: Transferrin deficiency affects iron transport and can lead to anemia, but it does not cause neurologic signs like numbness or gait abnormalities.

stem: A known case of ulcerative colitis is not responding to the anti-TNF alpha agent. Which JAK inhibitor is preferred for moderate to severe ulcerative colitis?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Tofacitinib is an oral JAK 1/3 Inhibitor that blocks the JAK-STAT pathway, reducing the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines involved in ulcerative colitis.
• It is indicated for moderate to severe ulcerative colitis, especially in patients not responding to anti-TNF alpha therapy.
• Tofacitinib provides a rapid onset of action and can be administered orally, making it convenient for long-term therapy.
• It is also approved for rheumatoid arthritis and psoriatic arthritis, reflecting its immunomodulatory effects.
• Regular monitoring of lipid profile, liver function, and complete blood count is recommended during therapy due to potential adverse effects.


Other Options:
Option A: Vedolizumab is an Integrin a4B7 Inhibitor used in ulcerative colitis but is not a JAK inhibitor. It is mainly preferred for anti-TNF failures but works via gut-selective lymphocyte trafficking Inhibition.
Option C: Ozanimod is a S1P receptor modulator, also used in moderate to severe UC, but it is not a JAK inhibitor.
Option D: Leflunomide is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) used in rheumatoid arthritis and some autoimmune diseases, not Indicated for ulcerative colitis.

stem: In children with GBS, all are true except?
options: Areflexia of the affected limb is a mandatory diagnostic criteria
Plasmapheresis is not preferred in refractory cases
Intravenous immunoglobulin is the first line of management
Albuminocytological dissociation in CSF is characteristic
subject: Pediatrics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Areflexia of affected limbs is a key diagnostic feature of GBS and is typically present in most cases.
• Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) is the first-line treatment for pediatric GBS and is preferred due to ease of administration and safety.
• Albuminocytological dissociation in CSF, characterized by elevated protein with normal cell count, is a classic diagnostic hallmark.
• Plasmapheresis is indeed preferred in refractory or severe cases of GBS when there is inadequate response to IVIG.
• Mandatory diagnostic criteria for GBS: 1. Progressive, symmetric muscle weakness of more than one limb: Weakness evolves over a period of hours to weeks. Typically starts in the legs and ascends (ascending paralysis). 2. Areflexia or hyporeflexia: Deep tendon reflexes are reduced or absent in affected limbs.
• Mechanism of Immunopathogenesis of Guillain-Barre Syndrome: 1. B cells recognize glycoconjugates on C. jejuni that cross-react with ganglioside present on Schwann cell surface and subjacent peripheral nerve myelin. 2. Most of the B cells are activated via a partially T cell-dependent route and secrete primarily IgG autoantibodies. 3. The activated B cells escape from Peyer's patches into regional lymph nodes. 4. Activated T cells also function to assist in the opening of the blood-nerve barrier. 5. The earliest changes in myelin consist of edema between myelin lamellae. 6. These effects are associated with activation of the C5b-C9 membrane attack complex.


Other Options:
Option A: True absence of reflexes (areflexia) is characteristic and helps confirm the diagnosis.
Option C: True - IVIG is effective in reducing disease duration and improving outcomes.
Option D: True - CSF shows albuminocytological dissociation, aiding in diagnosis.

stem: As per KDIGO, which of the following falls under G3b?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• G3b CKD corresponds to an eGFR of 30-44 mL/min/1.73 m², indicating moderately to severely decreased kidney function.
• GFR categories per KDIGO: 1. G1: >=90 (normal or high) 2. G2: 60-89 (mildly decreased) 3. G3a: 45-59 (mildly to moderately decreased) 4. G3b: 30-44 (moderately to severely decreased) 5. G4: 15-29 (severely decreased) 6. G5: <15 (kidney failure).
• Identification of G3b is important for timely intervention and slowing CKD progression.

Kidney Disease Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) stages of chronic kidney disease (CKD):
StageGFR

260 to 89 ml/min/1.73 m²
3a45 to 59 ml/min/1.73 m²
3b30 to 44 ml/min/1.73 m²
415 to 29 ml/min/1.73 m²
5 (end-stage renal disease)< 15 ml/min/1.73 m²



Other Options:
Option A: Falls under G3a, not G3b.
Option B: Falls under G2 (mild CKD).
Option D: Falls under G4 (severe CKD).

stem: A 46-year-old diabetic patient presented with fever, malaise, and a productive cough. His chest X-ray is given below. Which of the following is incorrect?
options: Patchy infiltrates as seen in pyogenic pneumonia
Interstitial infiltrates as seen in mycoplasma pneumonia
Hilar lymphadenopathy with infiltrates as seen in malignancy
Aspirational pneumonia is more common in the upper segment of the lower lobe and posterior segment of the upper lobe
subject: Medicine, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Pyogenic (typical) pneumonia usually presents with lobar consolidation, not patchy infiltrates.
• Patchy infiltrates are more characteristic of atypical pneumonia caused by Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, or viral infections.
• Hilar lymphadenopathy with infiltrates may be seen in malignancy or certain infections like TB.
• Aspirational pneumonia typically affects the upper segments of the lower lobes and posterior segments of the upper lobes, especially in recumbent patients.


Other Options:
Option B: Interstitial infiltrates are classic for mycoplasma or viral pneumonia.
Option C: Hilar lymphadenopathy is associated with lung malignancy or infections like tuberculosis.
Option D: Aspiration pneumonia pattern involves the posterior upper lobes and superior segments of lower lobes due to gravity in supine position.

stem: A patient presented with uveitis, enthesitis, and sacroiliitis. Which of the following is most likely associated with this condition?
subject: Medicine, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Uveitis, enthesitis, and sacroiliitis are hallmark features of ankylosing spondylitis (AS), a type of seronegative spondyloarthropathy.
• Ankylosing spondylitis is strongly associated with the HLA-B27 antigen.
• Other conditions associated with HLA-B27 include reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease-associated arthritis.
• HLA testing is often used to support the diagnosis of these conditions, especially in cases with ambiguous clinical presentations.
