stem: A 25-year-old male comes with hematuria. Which of the following are seen in glomerular hematuria? 1. Brown-coloured urine 2. Dysuria absent 3. Crystals present 4. Edema rare
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Brown-colored urine occurs due to hemoglobin or myoglobin from glomerular bleeding.
• Dysuria is absent in glomerular hematuria because the bleeding originates from the glomerulus, not the lower urinary tract.
• Edema is rare in isolated glomerular hematuria unless associated with nephritic syndrome.
• Other features may include red blood cell casts and dysmorphic RBCs on microscopy, which indicate glomerular origin.
• Glomerular hematuria is the presence of red blood cells (RBCs) in the urine that originate from the glomeruli. It can be identified by the presence of either of the following: 1. Dysmorphic RBCs (>40%) 2. Acanthocytes (>5%) 3. RBC Casts (>1).


Other Options:
Option A: Only brown-colored urine is insufficient; dysuria absence and rare edema are also characteristic.
Option B: Ignores that edema is rare, which is an important distinguishing feature.
Option C: Crystals are not seen in glomerular hematuria; their presence usually indicates urinary tract stones.

stem: A male patient presented with oral ulcers and ulcers in the anal canal. He was diagnosed with Behcet's disease. Which of the following is not seen in this condition?
options: Ulcer over the dorsum of the penis
It is similar to Crohn's disease than ulcerative colitis
Pathergy test positive is a criterion of ISG diagnostic criteria for Behcet's
subject: Medicine, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Genital ulcers in Behcet's disease typically occur on the scrotum in men and the vulva in women; ulcers over the dorsum of the penis are not seen.
• Behcet's disease is a systemic vasculitis that can present with recurrent oral ulcers, genital ulcers, skin lesions, ocular involvement, and gastrointestinal lesions, often mimicking Crohn's disease more than ulcerative colitis.
• Bilateral panuveitis is a common ocular manifestation, leading to vision complications if untreated.
• Pathergy test positivity is part of the International Study Group (ISG) diagnostic criteria for Behcet's disease and supports the diagnosis.


Other Options:
Option B: Gastrointestinal involvement in Behcet's resembles Crohn's disease, with ulcers in the ileocecal region, not like ulcerative colitis.
Option C: Ocular involvement such as bilateral panuveitis is characteristic of Behcet's.
Option D: Pathergy test is a minor but diagnostic criterion; positive test indicates hyper-reactivity of the skin.

stem: The female patient comes to the ER with palpitations and syncopal attacks following exercise. She has had similar episodes in the past. An ECG was done as follows. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia (ARVD) is characterized by fibrofatty replacement of the right ventricular myocardium, predisposing to ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death, especially in young adults during exertion.
• ECG findings in ARVD include epsilon waves in leads V1-V3, T wave inversions in the right precordial leads, and ventricular arrhythmias of left bundle branch block morphology.
• Clinical features include palpitations, syncope, and exercise-induced arrhythmias. ARVD is an inherited cardiomyopathy, usually autosomal dominant with variable penetrance.


Other Options:
Option A: Rare congenital absence of the right ventricular myocardium, presents with right heart failure, but not classically with arrhythmias or epsilon waves.
Option C: Presents with exertional syncope and sudden death, but ECG shows LV hypertrophy, deep Q waves, and repolarization abnormalities, not epsilon waves.
Option D: ECG shows ST elevation in V1-V3 with a coved pattern, often causing sudden death, but structural abnormalities are absent.

stem: A 20-year-old male patient presented with a history of occasional palpitation, high BP, and headache. The patient was diagnosed with pheochromocytoma. Which of the following will be the preferred first investigation?
subject: Medicine, Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• 24-hour urine metanephrines is the preferred first-line investigation for pheochromocytoma due to its high sensitivity and specificity.
• Pheochromocytoma produces catecholamines, which are metabolized into metanephrines and normetanephrines; these metabolites are measured in urine.
• Elevated urine metanephrines confirm the biochemical diagnosis of pheochromocytoma. After biochemical confirmation, imaging studies (MRI abdomen, CT, or MIBG scan) are performed to localize the tumor.
• This approach minimizes unnecessary imaging before confirming the functional tumor.
• Pheochromocytoma Features: 1. When pheochromocytoma is associated with the MEN syndrome it is rarely malignant. 2. Phaeochromocytomas are greyish-pink on the cut surface and are usually highly vascularised. 3. Zellballen cells and nuclei with salt and pepper chromatin are seen.
• Diagnosis: 1. Measurement of the plasma metanephrines is the most sensitive screening test. 2. MRI is the IOC. 3. Biopsy and CT Contrast scans are contraindicated in order to avoid a hypertensive crisis.
• Treatment: 1. Adrenalectomy is preferred TOC 2. Size<5 cm-Laparoscopic adrenalectomy 3. Preop Alpha blockers should be given.


Other Options:
Option A: US-abdomen is useful for initial localization but less sensitive, especially for small adrenal tumors.
Option B: MIBG is primarily used after biochemical confirmation for localization or to detect extra-adrenal or metastatic pheochromocytoma.
Option C: MRI-abdomen is highly sensitive for localization, but not the first-line test without prior biochemical confirmation.

stem: Marshall's triad (punctate lacerations, bruises, and abrasions) is seen in which type of blast injury?
subject: FMT, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Secondary blast injury is caused by projectiles or fragments propelled by the explosion.
• Marshall's triad-punctate lacerations, bruises, and abrasions-is characteristic of secondary blast injuries.
• These injuries occur when shrapnel, debris, or broken glass strikes the body.
• Secondary blast injuries can affect any part of the body and are often penetrating or blunt in nature.
• Recognition of Marshall's triad helps forensic experts identify blast-related trauma.
Blast Injury typeMechanism of InjuryHealth Impact
PrimaryRapid crushing over pressureTympanic membrane (most common), Lung (2nd most common), eyes, GIT
SecondaryFlying debrisMARSHAL's triad (triad of abrasions, contusions, lacerations)
TertiaryBlast windVictim is thrown in air and strikes other objects leading to fracture and blunt trauma
QuaternaryAny complicating factor not in the other three categoriesBurns, histotoxic anoxia, and crush injuries



Other Options:
Option A: Primary blast injury is caused by the blast wave itself, typically affecting air-containing organs like lungs, ears, and gastrointestinal tract, not associated with Marshall's triad.
Option C: Tertiary blast injury results from the victim being thrown by the blast wind, leading to blunt trauma, fractures, or contusions, but not the punctate lacerations of Marshall's triad.
Option D: Quaternary blast injury includes burns, inhalational injuries, and exacerbation of pre-existing medical conditions, unrelated to Marshall's triad.

stem: What is not seen in heat exhaustion?
subject: Medicine, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Heat exhaustion occurs due to prolonged exposure to high temperatures, causing dehydration and electrolyte imbalance.
• Thermoregulatory mechanisms are intact, so the body can still sweat and attempt to cool itself.
• Patients may present with altered sensorium (dizziness, confusion), orthostatic hypotension, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, headache, and thirst.
• Core body temperature is usually elevated but does not exceed 105°F (40.5°C).
• Prompt intervention with cooling and rehydration is necessary to prevent progression to heat stroke.
Hyperthermic disordersMechanisms
Heat edemaCutaneous vasodilation and pooling of interstitial fluid leads to swelling of hands, feet, and ankles.
Heat syncopeVasodilation and volume depletion (from sweating) following exercise or prolonged standing with consequent systemic and cerebral hypotension.
Heat crampsDeficiency of sodium, potassium, and water at an intracellular level, leading to intermittent, painful, and involuntary spasmodic contractions of skeletal muscles.
Heat exhaustionResults from prolonged strenuous activity. There is water or salt depletion, but thermoregulatory mechanisms are maintained. Temperature <40.5°C.
HeatstrokeThermoregulatory mechanisms are lost. Temperature >40.5°C



Other Options:
Option B: Altered sensorium is common in heat exhaustion due to dehydration and electrolyte imbalance.
Option C: Orthostatic hypotension occurs from fluid loss and hypovolemia.
Option D: Core body temperature remains below 105°F; temperatures above this suggest heat stroke rather than heat exhaustion.

stem: Cytochrome C oxidase present in the ETC can be blocked by all except?
subject: Biochemistry, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Cytochrome C oxidase (Complex IV) is the terminal enzyme of the mitochondrial electron transport chain responsible for reducing oxygen to water.
• Carbon monoxide, cyanide, and hydrogen sulphide inhibit Complex IV by binding to the heme iron, preventing electron transfer and cellular respiration.
• Nitric oxide can transiently modulate ETC activity but does not directly or permanently block Complex IV, so it is not a classical inhibitor.
• Inhibition of Complex IV by CO, CN, or H2S leads to histotoxic hypoxia, where tissues cannot utilize oxygen despite normal oxygen levels.
• Symptoms of poisoning include cyanosis, dyspnea, confusion, and lactic acidosis, depending on the toxin.


Other Options:
Option A: Carbon monoxide binds to heme iron in cytochrome C oxidase, blocking electron transport.
Option B: Cyanide binds strongly to ferric iron in Complex IV, causing rapid cellular hypoxia.
Option D: Hydrogen sulphide inhibits cytochrome C oxidase by binding to the enzyme's metal center, preventing oxidative phosphorylation.

stem: Correctly matched antidote with toxicity?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Flumazenil is a specific antidote for benzodiazepine overdose, acting as a competitive antagonist at the GABA-A receptor benzodiazepine binding site.
• It reverses sedation, respiratory depression, and CNS depression caused by benzodiazepines.
• Flumazenil should be used cautiously in chronic benzodiazepine users to avoid seizures.
• Other antidotes listed in options are incorrectly matched with their toxic agents.


Other Options:
Option A: Organophosphorus poisoning is treated with atropine (blocks muscarinic effects) and pralidoxime (reactivates acetylcholinesterase), not physostigmine.
Option B: Atropine overdose is managed supportively, sometimes with physostigmine, not L-carnitine.
Option D: Arsenic poisoning is treated with dimercaprol, DMSA, or DMPS, not desferrioxamine (which is used for iron toxicity).

stem: The compound given in the image below can be used in the treatment of which of the following poisonings?
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The compound shown is copper sulphate, which acts as an antidote for acute phosphorus poisoning.
• Phosphorus poisoning commonly occurs via ingestion of white phosphorus, often from rodenticides or matches.
• Early symptoms include abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, and a characteristic garlic-like odor in breath and excreta.
• Severe cases may show luminescent vomitus or stool ("smoky stool syndrome"), shock, hematemesis, hepatic failure, and renal insufficiency.
• Copper sulphate acts by precipitating phosphorus, reducing systemic absorption and toxicity.
FeaturesPhosphorus
Chemistry2 forms - White and Red Phosphorus. White Phosphorus is toxic, Red Phosphorus is not.
Mechanism of actionProtoplasmic irritant poison
Organs affectedLiver, Bone, Kidneys
Characteristic clinical findingsSmoky stool syndrome, Luminescent stools, Garlicky odor, Liver necrosis, Phossy jaw
TreatmentSymptomatic therapy


Other Options:
Option A: Aluminium phosphide poisoning is treated with magnesium sulphate, supportive therapy, and there is no role for copper sulphate.
Option B: Mercury poisoning is managed with chelating agents like dimercaprol, DMSA, not copper sulphate.
Option D: Arsenic poisoning is treated with dimercaprol or DMSA, not copper sulphate.

stem: A student consumed an unknown substance and came with shock and pulmonary edema. The silver nitrate breath test is positive. What is the antidote?
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The patient's presentation of shock, pulmonary edema, and positive silver nitrate breath test is highly suggestive of aluminium phosphide (ALP) poisoning.
• ALP releases phosphine gas, which is rapidly absorbed via the lungs and GI tract, leading to oxidative stress, myocardial toxicity, and multi-organ failure.
• Magnesium sulphate (MgSO4) is administered to correct hypomagnesemia, stabilize cardiac membranes, prevent arrhythmias, and reduce organ toxicity.
• Supportive therapy including fluids, inotropes, and oxygen is also essential, as no specific chelating agent exists for ALP.
• Silver Nitrate Test for Aluminium Phosphide (ALP) Poisoning:
• Purpose: Detects ALP poisoning.
• Background: ALP, used as a pesticide, releases phosphine gas when in contact with moisture. Phosphine blackens silver nitrate-impregnated paper.
• Methods: Gastric Aspirate: 1. Mix 5 mL gastric aspirate + 15 mL water in a flask. 2. Cover with silver nitrate paper and heat at 50°C for 15-20 min. 3. Black paper = presence of phosphine. Breath Test: 1. The patient breathes through a silver nitrate-impregnated face mask. 2. Black mask = phosphine detected (positive only if >6 g ALP ingested).


Other Options:
Option A: BAL (British anti-Lewisite) is an antidote for arsenic, mercury, and lead poisoning, not ALP.
Option B: Pralidoxime is used in organophosphate poisoning, not ALP.
Option D: Atropine is used to treat organophosphate or carbamate poisoning, not ALP.

stem: Identify the procedure:
subject: ENT, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Stapedotomy is performed for otosclerosis, where fixation of the stapes footplate causes conductive hearing loss.
• In this procedure, the stapes footplate is preserved, and only a small fenestra is created in it.
• A prosthetic piston is inserted through the fenestra, connecting the incus to the vestibule to restore sound transmission.
• It reduces inner ear trauma compared to stapedectomy and has better postoperative hearing outcomes.
• Stapedotomy is currently the preferred surgical technique over stapedectomy in otosclerosis.
• Contraindications for stapedotomy: Only hearing ear, Positive Schwartze sign, Otitis external, Meniere's disease (Can damage the inner ear while drilling a hole in the footplate), Young children, Professional athletes, pilots, construction workers, and divers: Sudden changes in pressure can displace the piston and cause vertigo. Those who work in noisy surroundings, as surgery, makes them more vulnerable to noise-induced hearing loss.


Other Options:
Option B: Myringoplasty involves repair of the tympanic membrane only and does not involve the stapes or prosthesis.
Option C: Tympanoplasty refers to reconstruction of the tympanic membrane with or without ossicular reconstruction but does not include stapes fenestration.
Option D: Ossiculoplasty involves reconstruction of the ossicular chain but does not specifically involve creating a fenestra in the stapes footplate.

stem: What is the most commonly used test in screening for neonatal hearing?
subject: ENT, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Otoacoustic emissions (OAE) assess the functional integrity of outer hair cells in the cochlea, which are affected early in sensorineural hearing loss.
• OAE is non-invasive, quick, cost-effective, and does not require sedation, making it ideal for newborn screening.
• It is the first-line screening test used in universal neonatal hearing screening programs.
• Babies who fail OAE screening are further evaluated using BERA/ABR, which is the confirmatory diagnostic test.
• Neonates admitted to the NICU are directly screened with BERA due to higher risk of auditory neuropathy.
• BERA (Brainstem Evoked Response Audiometry) or ABR (Auditory Brainstem Response) It is an objective test that records electrical activity occurring in the auditory pathway. Normally, 7 waves are produced in the first 10ms. Wave I-distal part of the cochlear nerve, Wave II-proximal part of the cochlear nerve, Wave III-cochlear nucleus, Wave IV-superior olivary complex, Wave V-lateral lemniscus, Wave VI & VII-inferior colliculus.
• BERA Findings: Conductive/Cochlear pathology: 1. Delayed Wave I 2. Normal inter-peak latency (I-V). Retrocochlear pathology: 1. Wave I present 2. Waves II-V absent. Acoustic neuroma/Vestibular schwannoma: 1. Prolonged inter-peak latency (I-V) 2. Increased inter-aural latency of Wave V (0.2 ms).
• Pure tone audiometry (PTA): A pure tone is a sound of a single frequency. An audiometer generates pure tones with intensity adjustable in 5 dB steps. Normal thresholds: For AC: 15 dB. For BC: 25 dB.
DiseaseFindings in PTA audiogram
Conductive hearing lossAC abnormal, BC normal, air-bone gap present
Sensorineural hearing lossBoth AC and BC are abnormal and no air-Bone gap
Mixed hearing lossBoth AC and BC abnormal and air-bone gap present
Presbycusis, ototoxicity, noise-induced hearing loss (higher frequencies affected first)Downsloping or descending audiogram
Early stages of Meniere's disease (lower frequencies affected first)Upsloping or ascending audiogram
Congenital hearing loss and cochlear otosclerosis (mid-frequency hearing loss)U-shaped or trough-shaped or cookie bite shaped audiogram
Early cases of noise-induced hearing lossAcoustic dip-characteristic dip in both AC and BC at 4000 Hz
OtosclerosisCarhart's notch-dip at 2000 Hz in BC curve


Other Options:
Option A: Conduction audiometry requires active patient cooperation and is not suitable for neonates.
Option C: BERA is a definitive diagnostic test, not commonly used for initial screening in all newborns.
Option D: Clap and smile is an obsolete and unreliable method of hearing assessment.

stem: Identify the type of hearing loss:
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Mixed hearing loss involves pathology affecting both the conductive mechanism (external or middle ear) and the sensorineural pathway (inner ear or auditory nerve).
• In audiometry, it is characterized by elevated air-conduction thresholds along with elevated bone-conduction thresholds, with a persistent air-bone gap.
• This pattern indicates that sound transmission as well as cochlear/nerve function are impaired.
• Common clinical conditions associated with mixed hearing loss include chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM) with inner ear damage and otosclerosis with cochlear involvement.
• Identification of mixed hearing loss is important because management often requires both medical/surgical treatment and hearing rehabilitation.
• Pure tone audiometry (PTA) A pure tone is a sound of a single frequency. An audiometer generates pure tones with intensity adjustable in 5 dB steps. It measures hearing thresholds for 1. Air conduction (AC): 125-8000 Hz 2. Bone conduction (BC): 250-4000 Hz Results are plotted on an audiogram. Normal thresholds: For AC: 15 dB For BC: 25 dB.
DiseaseFindings in PTA audiogram
Conductive hearing lossAC abnormal, BC normal, air-bone gap present
Sensorineural hearing lossBoth AC and BC are abnormal and no air-Bone gap
Mixed hearing lossBoth AC and BC abnormal and air-bone gap present
Presbycusis, ototoxicity, noise-induced hearing loss (higher frequencies affected first)Downsloping or descending audiogram
Early stages of Meniere's disease (lower frequencies affected first)Upsloping or ascending audiogram
Congenital hearing loss and cochlear otosclerosis (mid-frequency hearing loss)U-shaped or trough-shaped or cookie bite shaped audiogram
Early cases of noise-induced hearing lossAcoustic dip-characteristic dip in both AC and BC at 4000 Hz
OtosclerosisCarhart's notch-dip at 2000 Hz in BC curve


Other Options:
Option B: Conductive hearing loss shows normal bone conduction with elevated air conduction only.
Option C: Sensory (sensorineural) hearing loss shows equal elevation of air and bone conduction without an air-bone gap.
Option D: Normal hearing shows air and bone conduction thresholds within normal limits.

stem: The posterior end of the marked structure is present at the level of which of the following?
subject: Anatomy, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• The marked structure refers to the inferior turbinate, which is the largest turbinate on the lateral wall of the nasal cavity.
• The posterior end of the inferior turbinate lies opposite the opening of the Eustachian tube in the lateral wall of the nasopharynx.
• The Eustachian tube connects the nasopharynx to the middle ear, helping in pressure equalization and drainage of middle ear secretions.
• The pharyngeal opening of the Eustachian tube is located approximately 1-1.25 cm posterior to the posterior end of the inferior turbinate.
• Woodruff's venous plexus, a common source of posterior epistaxis in adults, lies just below the posterior end of the inferior turbinate, further confirming the anatomical relationship.


Other Options:
Option B: The sphenopalatine foramen is located in the posterior superior part of the lateral nasal wall, not at the level of the inferior turbinate.
Option C: The odontoid process is part of the axis (C2 vertebra) and has no direct relation to nasal cavity anatomy.
Option D: The sphenoidal sinus opens into the sphenoethmoidal recess, which lies superior to the superior turbinate.

stem: Carhart's notch is seen in
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Carhart's notch is a specific dip in bone conduction threshold at 2000 Hz on pure tone audiometry.
• It is characteristically seen in otosclerosis, caused by stapes footplate fixation in the middle ear.
• The notch occurs due to reduced inertial bone conduction resulting from stapes immobilization.
• Surgical treatment (stapedotomy/stapedectomy) often improves the bone conduction threshold and partially resolves the notch.
• It is not seen in sensorineural hearing loss conditions like noise-induced hearing loss or Meniere's disease.
• Pure Tone Audiometry In Otosclerosis: Air conduction is poor & worse for lower frequencies. Large A-B gap. Bone conduction is normal. In some cases, there is a dip in the bone conduction curve. It is different at different frequencies but is maximum at 2000 Hz and is called Carhart's notch.


Other Options:
Option B: Noise-induced hearing loss typically shows a high-frequency notch at 3-6 kHz, not at 2 kHz.
Option C: Meniere's disease presents with low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, not a Carhart's notch.
Option D: Serous otitis media shows a conductive hearing loss but does not produce a specific Carhart's notch.

stem: Which of the following is the most dangerous lesion?
subject: ENT, Surgery, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid, which is supplied by the superior laryngeal nerve (SLN).
• Bilateral complete RLN lesion causes paralysis of all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except cricothyroid, leading to severe airway compromise.
Type of palsyNerveCord positionVoiceTreatment
U/L AbductorRLN (left)ParamedianHoarseWait & watch (palsy with least consequences)
B/L AbductorBoth RLNsBoth in ParamedianGoodTracheostomy, lateralization
U/L completeRLN+SLN (left)CadavericHoarseWait & watch, medialization
B/L completeBoth RLN+SLNCadaveric (Intermediate) positionAphoniaTracheostomy + Epiglottopexy

PositionDistance from medianSeen In
Median0During speech/ swallowing
Paramedian1 mmRecurrent laryngeal nerve palsy
Intermediate/Cadaveric3 mmComplete paralysis
Slight abduction7 mmNormal respiration
Full abduction9 mmStrained respiration


Other Options:
Option A: Unilateral partial RLN lesion causes mild voice changes but rarely compromises airway.
Option B: Bilateral partial RLN lesion may cause hoarseness and mild dyspnea, but airway is usually adequate.
Option C: Unilateral complete RLN lesion results in hoarseness and weak voice but does not threaten airway.

stem: Identify the nasal fracture:
subject: ENT, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Chevallet fracture occurs due to a blow to the nose from below, producing a vertical fracture line in the nasal septum.
• The fracture line extends from the anterior nasal spine upwards to the junction of the bony and cartilaginous dorsum of the nose.
• It may involve buckling, vertical splitting, or crushing of the septum depending on the trauma.
• This fracture is distinct from horizontal fractures and requires careful assessment to prevent nasal deformity and obstruction.
• Early identification is important to guide reduction or surgical repair.


Other Options:
Option B: Jarjaway fracture is horizontal, caused by a frontal blow, not vertical.
Option C: Simple fractures involve minimal displacement, unlike Chevallet's vertical septal fracture.
Option D: Le Fort fractures involve the maxilla and midface, not the nasal septum.

stem: What causes maximum hearing loss?
options: Malleus-incus discontinuity with intact tympanic membrane
Malleus-incus discontinuity with perforation of tympanic membrane
Partial fixation of the footplate of stapes
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Malleus-incus discontinuity with intact tympanic membrane results in complete disruption of the ossicular chain, blocking sound transmission to the cochlea.
• An intact tympanic membrane prevents any alternative sound conduction, worsening conductive hearing loss.
• This condition can lead to maximum conductive hearing loss up to 60 dB.
• Early surgical intervention (ossiculoplasty) can restore hearing. Hearing loss severity depends on the integrity of both the ossicular chain and tympanic membrane.
LesionHearing Loss
Complete obstruction of the ear canal30 dB
Perforation of tympanic membrane10-40 dB
Ossicular interruption with intact drum54 dB
Ossicular interruption with perforation38 dB
Malleus fixation10-25 dB
Closure of oval window60 dB


Other Options:
Option B: With tympanic membrane perforation, some sound can still bypass the ossicular discontinuity, so hearing loss is less severe.
Option C: Partial fixation of the stapes footplate causes moderate conductive hearing loss, not maximal.
Option D: Otitis media with effusion usually causes mild to moderate conductive hearing loss, less than ossicular discontinuity.

stem: Ambu Aura 40 is an example of?
subject: Anaesthesia, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The Ambu Aura 40 is a first-generation supraglottic airway device designed to provide a simple and reliable airway for ventilation without an endotracheal tube.
• First-generation devices do not have a gastric access channel, and their primary function is basic ventilation.
• They are easy to insert and are commonly used in elective surgeries, emergency airway management, and as a bridge to definitive airway.
• Second-generation devices include features like gastric access and higher sealing pressures for improved safety.
• The Ambu Aura 40 is lightweight, anatomically curved, and widely used in routine airway management.


Other Options:
Option B: Generation 2 devices, like LMA ProSeal, have a gastric channel to reduce aspiration risk.
Option C: Generation 3 devices are newer, specialized supraglottic devices with enhanced sealing and intubation capabilities.
Option D: Generation 4 devices do not exist in the standard classification, most current devices are first or second generation.

stem: Which of the following drugs causes adrenal suppression?
subject: Pharmacology, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Etomidate inhibits 11B-hydroxylase, an enzyme required for the synthesis of cortisol and aldosterone in the adrenal cortex.
• This inhibition leads to adrenal suppression, which can persist for up to 72 hours after a single dose.
• The resulting decrease in cortisol can impair the stress response, particularly in critically ill or septic patients.
• Etomidate is preferred for hemodynamically unstable patients for induction due to minimal cardiovascular effects, but adrenal suppression is a major limitation.
• Cortisol precursors, such as 11-deoxycorticosterone, accumulate due to the enzymatic blockade.


Other Options:
Option A: Thiopental does not cause adrenal suppression; it primarily acts as a GABA agonist for induction.
Option B: Ketamine stimulates sympathetic activity and does not inhibit adrenal function.
Option D: Propofol has no significant effect on adrenal steroidogenesis.

stem: Which of the following is incorrect regarding thiopentone?
options: It achieves maximum concentration in the brain after an IV infusion
Redistribution to adipose tissue and muscle is seen
Rapid decline in its effect is due to renal excretion
subject: Pharmacology, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Thiopentone is highly lipid-soluble, allowing rapid penetration of the blood-brain barrier and fast onset of action.
• Following intravenous administration, it achieves maximum concentration in the brain within seconds due to high perfusion.
• The rapid decline in clinical effect is primarily due to redistribution from the brain to less perfused tissues such as muscle and adipose tissue, not due to metabolism or renal excretion.
• Hepatic metabolism (via oxidation and conjugation) eventually clears thiopentone from the body over several hours.
• Redistribution explains the short duration of action after a single IV bolus despite slower metabolic clearance.


Other Options:
Option A: True - Thiopentone is highly lipid-soluble, enabling fast CNS penetration.
Option B: True.
Option C: True - Peak brain concentration occurs almost immediately after IV administration. Redistribution to muscle and fat accounts for rapid decline in effect.

stem: A female following an RTA injured her chest. On X-ray, a fracture of 3rd to 5th ribs was noted. Pain was not responsive to analgesics. Which block will be given to reduce the pain?
subject: Anaesthesia, Surgery, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Rib fractures, especially of 3rd to 5th ribs, can cause severe pain leading to splinting, hypoventilation, and hypoxia.
• Thoracic epidural block provides segmental analgesia to thoracic dermatomes corresponding to the fractured ribs.
• Local anesthetics injected into the thoracic epidural space block the transmission of pain signals from the affected thoracic nerves.
• This block improves respiratory mechanics and prevents complications like atelectasis and pneumonia.
• It is preferred for severe rib fracture pain unresponsive to systemic analgesics.
• Thoracic epidurals are also used for postoperative pain and chronic thoracic pain syndromes.


Other Options:
Option A: It covers neck and upper limb, not thoracic ribs.
Option C: It targets brachial plexus; not suitable for rib pain.
Option D: It covers lower limbs and lower abdomen, not thoracic dermatomes.

stem: Which of the following will lead to a decrease in diastolic BP?
options: Systemic infusion of smooth muscle relaxant
Systemic infusion of beta-1 adrenergic agonist
subject: Physiology, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Diastolic blood pressure (DBP) is largely determined by vascular tone, which is maintained by sympathetic alpha-adrenergic activity.
• Loss of lumbar sympathetic outflow (as occurs in spinal Anaesthesia) causes vasodilation in both arterial and venous beds.
• Venous capacitance vessels are more affected, leading to blood pooling, reduced venous return, and decreased cardiac preload.
• The reduction in vascular tone directly lowers diastolic pressure.
• Clinically, patients may develop orthostatic hypotension, lightheadedness, or syncope due to decreased DBP.
• This mechanism explains why spinal or epidural Anaesthesia often causes hypotension.


Other Options:
Option A: It primarily decreases systemic vascular resistance; may lower DBP but the effect is gradual and dose-dependent.
Option B: It mainly affects skeletal or visceral smooth muscle, not systemic vascular tone significantly.
Option D: It increases cardiac contractility and heart rate; diastolic BP is minimally affected.

stem: Which of the following is true regarding paediatric cardiopulmonary resuscitation?
options: Chest compression at the rate of 30:2 with 2 rescuers
Adenosine via interosseous is preferred over other methods
In chest compression, depth must be at least 1/3rd of the child's chest AP diameter
Adrenaline given in dose 0.01 mg/kg iv/io in 1:1000 concentration
subject: Pediatrics, Anaesthesia, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• In paediatric CPR, the depth of chest compressions should be at least 1/3rd of the anteroposterior (AP) diameter of the chest.
• This ensures adequate blood flow to vital organs during resuscitation.
• For infants, this corresponds to roughly 1.5 inches (4 cm); for older children, it increases proportionally.
• Maintaining correct depth is more important than just the rate to ensure effective perfusion.
• Infant/Pediatric Chest Compressions: Rate: 100-120/min (30 compressions per 15-18 sec) allowing full chest recoil between compressions.
• Technique: Single rescuer: Two fingers, 1 cm below nipple line. Two rescuers: Hands encircle chest, thumbs 1 cm below nipples. Depth: 1.5 in or 1/3 chest depth.


Other Options:
Option A: For two-rescuer CPR, the compression-to-ventilation ratio is 15:2, not 30:2.
Option B: Adenosine can be given IV/IO for tachyarrhythmias, but the interosseous route is not universally preferred over IV.
Option D: Adrenaline is given at 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO, but in 1:10,000 concentration, not 1:1,000.

stem: Which of the following must be avoided in post-cardiac arrest resuscitation care?
options: Revascularisation procedure if the patient develops ST elevation post-resuscitation
Bring the body temperature down to below 32 degrees, therapeutic hypothermia if unresponsive
subject: Medicine, Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Targeted temperature management (TTM) in post-cardiac arrest care involves maintaining a body temperature between 32°C and 36°C for at least 24 hours.
• Cooling below 32°C (deep hypothermia) can lead to arrhythmias, coagulopathy, and other complications.
• Maintaining MAP >65 mmHg ensures adequate cerebral and systemic perfusion.
• Maintaining SpO2 above 94% avoids hypoxia and ensures optimal oxygen delivery.
• Early coronary revascularization is indicated if ST-elevation myocardial infarction is suspected after resuscitation.


Other Options:
Option A: Maintaining MAP >65 mmHg is recommended to support organ perfusion.
Option B: SpO2 >94% helps prevent hypoxic injury.
Option C: Immediate revascularization is indicated in ST-elevation post-arrest to improve outcomes.

stem: Jackson Rees circuit is used in:
subject: Anaesthesia, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The Jackson Rees circuit is a modification of the Ayre's T-piece, classified as a Mapleson F system (semi-closed circuit).
• It is commonly used in paediatric anaesthesia for children <6 years or <20 kg due to its low resistance and minimal dead space.
• Provides controlled or spontaneous ventilation safely in small children. Allows easy scavenging of CO2 and rapid changes in anesthetic depth.
• Lightweight and simple, making it ideal for short procedures and transport of pediatric patients.

Other Options:
Option B: Cardiac anaesthesia uses circle or specialized ventilator circuits, not Jackson Rees.
Option C: Neuro anaesthesia requires precise control, often with circle system or ventilators.
Option D: Obstetric anaesthesia uses standard circle system or spinal/epidural; Jackson Rees is not used.

stem: What is the mechanism responsible for the release of secretions from the gland seen in the image below?
subject: Anatomy, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The gland shown in the image is a sebaceous gland, typically associated with hair follicles.
• Holocrine secretion involves the entire cell disintegrating to release its lipid-rich contents, which forms sebum.
• Sebum lubricates the skin and hair, providing a protective barrier.
• Holocrine secretion contrasts with apocrine (apical cytoplasm pinched off), merocrine/eccrine (exocytosis without loss of cell), and other mechanisms.
• Sebaceous glands are mainly found in the face, scalp, upper chest, and back, areas rich in pilosebaceous units.


Other Options:
Option A: Apocrine glands release part of the cell cytoplasm with secretions (e.g., axilla, groin).
Option B: Merocrine/eccrine glands secrete via exocytosis, without cell loss (e.g., sweat glands).
Option C: Eccrine is a subtype of merocrine secretion, mainly for thermoregulation.

stem: All are a part of NAME syndrome, a subset of Carney's complex, except?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• NAME syndrome is a subset of Carney Complex and stands for Nevi, Atrial myxoma, Myxold neurofibroma, Ephelides (freckling).
• Atrial myxomas are characteristic cardiac tumors seen in Carney Complex and NAME syndrome.
• Nevi (pigmented skin lesions) and myxold neurofibromas are also components of NAME syndrome.
• The syndrome is caused by mutations in the PRKAR1A gene and manifests with multiple neoplasms, skin pigmentation. and endocrine tumors.
• Ebstein anomaly (a congenital heart defect of the tricuspid valve) is not associated with NAME syndrome.
• Carney complex is also known as NAME syndrome and LAMB syndrome: Carney's Complex: Rare autosomal dominant disorder with multiple neoplasms and pigmentation abnormalities.
• LAMB: Lentigines, Atrial Myxoma, Blue nevi NAME (subse of Carney's): 1. N-Nevi 2. A-Atrial myxomas 3. M-Myxoid neurofibromas 4. E-Ephelides (freckle-like lesions).


Other Options:
Option A: Atrial myxoma is a hallmark cardiac feature of NAME syndrome.
Option B: Nevi represents the cutaneous pigmented lesions seen in NAME syndrome.
Option D: Myxold neurofibromas are soft tissue tumors included in NAME syndrome.

stem: A 60-year-old woman presented with a great toe infection. She had significant discoloration of the toenail with subungual hyperkeratosis that caused color alteration and a rough surface area. She was suspected to have onychomycosis. Which topical drug is given for toenail onychomycosis?
subject: Dermatology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Tavaborole is a topical antifungal specifically approved for the treatment of toenail onychomycosis.
• It belongs to the oxaborole class and inhibits fungal protein synthesis by targeting leucyl-tRNA synthetase.
• Onychomycosis is a fungal infection of the nail plate, often caused by Trichophyton rubrum, though other dermatophytes and non-dermatophyte molds may also be responsible.
• Topical therapy like Tavaborole is preferred in mild-to-moderate distal subungual onychomycosis without extensive nail involvement.
• Clinical signs include discoloration, subungual hyperkeratosis, and roughened nall surfaces.


Other Options:
Option A: Caspofungin is an intravenous echinocandin, not used topically for nails.
Option B: Itraconazole is systemic and oral; not a first-line topical for toenail onychomycosis.
Option C: Griseofulvin is oral and less effective for toenail infections, not used topically.

stem: A male patient presents with urethritis. It can be caused by all except?
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Gardnerella vaginalls is primarily associated with bacterial vaginosis in females and is not a causative agent of urethritis in males.
• Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes gonococcal urethritis, the most common cause of acute urethritis in males.
• Trichomonas vaginalis can cause non-gonococcal urethritis in males, usually presenting with urethral discharge and dysuria.
• Mycoplasma specles (e.g., M. genitalium, M. hominis) are well-recognized causes of non-gonococcal urethritis in men.
• Male urethritis is classified into gonococcal and non-gonococcal, with Gardnerella vaginalis not Implicated In either type.


Other Options:
Option A: Neisseria gonorrhoeae is the classic causative agent of gonococcal urethritis.
Option C: Trichomonas vaginalis can cause non-gonococcal urethritis in males.
Option D: Mycoplasma species are important causes of non-gonococcal urethritis.

stem: A 60-year-old female comes with a complaint of being unable to bear weight following trivial trauma. X-ray of hip and leg is given. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Orthopaedics, Radiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Elderly patients with trivial trauma leading to inability to bear weight should raise suspicion for pathological fractures.
• X-ray shows large osteolytic lesions which are common sites for metastatic deposits, especially in the pelvis and long bones.
• Metastatic lesions in the elderly are commonly due to breast, lung, thyrold, kidney, or prostate cancers.
• Features differentiating metastasis from other lesions include age, multiplicity, cortical destruction, and periosteal reaction pattern.
• Pathological Fracture: A fracture occurring in a structurally weakened bone, often spontaneous or after trivial trauma.
• Features include: Pain at the site preceding fracture, Multiple recent fractures, Elderly patient, History of primary malignancy, Unusual fracture pattern (eg, banana fracture-transverse fracture through abnormal bone).
1. Generalized bone disease:2. Local benign conditions:3. Primary malignant tumors:4. Metastatic tumors
*OsteoporosisChronic infectionChondrosarcomaMets from breast, lung, kidney, thyroid, colon and prostate
Metabolic bone diseasesSolitary bone cystOsteosarcoma
Osteogenesis ImperfectaAneurysmal bone cystEwing's tumor
Paget's diseaseChondroma
Polyostotic fibrous dysplasia
*Myelomatosis



Other Options:
Option A: Brown tumor is due to hyperparathyroidism, not typical in elderly trauma patients.
Option B: Disseminated TB rarely presents as multiple lytic lesions in the elderly without systemic signs.
Option C: Eosinophilic granuloma is usually a pediatric lesion and uncommon in elderly adults.

stem: A 40-year-old male comes with an intracapsular fracture of the femur. What is the surgery of choice?
subject: Orthopaedics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• In young and middle-aged adults (< 60-65 years), preservation of the femoral head is the primary goal.
• Closed reduction and Internal fixation (CRIF) using multiple cannulated cancellous screws is the treatment of choice.
• Closed reduction minimizes soft tissue disruption and preserves the blood supply to the femoral head, reducing the risk of avascular necrosis.
• Open reduction is associated with a higher complication rate and is reserved only when closed reduction falls.
• Arthroplasty procedures are avoided in younger patients due to Implant longevity Issues and higher functional demands.


Other Options:
Option A: Open reduction internal fixation is done only if closed reduction is unsuccessful; it carries a higher risk of avascular necrosis.
Option C: Hemlarthroplasty is preferred in elderly patients with low functional demand.
Option D: Total hip arthroplasty is indicated in elderly, active patients or when there is pre-existing hip arthritis.

stem: FRAX is related to?
subject: Orthopaedics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• FRAX (Fracture Risk Assessment Tool) is used to estimate the 10-year probability of osteoporotic fractures, including hip and major osteoporotic fractures.
• It integrates clinical risk factors such as age, sex, BMI, prior fracture, family history, smoking, alcohol intake, and glucocorticold use, with or without BMD.
• FRAX is particularly useful in postmenopausal women and men aged >=50 years.
• It helps clinicians decide when to Initiate anti-osteoporotic therapy, especially in patients with osteopenia.
• The tool is widely recommended by international osteoporosis guidelines for risk stratification and treatment planning.


Other Options:
Option B: Fracture rehabilitation assessment refers to post-fracture recovery evaluation, which is not the purpose of FRAX.
Option C: Fracture repair assessment relates to healing and union, not future fracture risk prediction.
Option D: Fracture reduction assessment involves acute fracture management, not long-term risk estimation.

stem: Which of the following is a complication of diabetic retinopathy?
subject: Ophthalmology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Diabetic retinopathy is a microvascular complication of diabetes that can lead to proliferative changes, including neovascularization.
• Tractional retinal detachment (TRD) occurs due to fibrovascular proliferation on the retina, which contracts and pulls the retina away from the underlying pigment epithellum.
• TRD is most commonly seen in proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) and can lead to severe vision loss if untreated.
• Early detection with fundus examination and timely laser photocoagulation or vitrectomy can prevent progression to TRD.
• Other complications of diabetic retinopathy include macular edema, vitreous hemorrhage, and neovascular glaucoma.


Other Options:
Option A: Posterior staphyloma is associated with high myopia, not diabetic retinopathy.
Option B: Retinoschisis is splitting of retinal layers, often seen in degenerative myopia or X-linked retinoschisis.
Option D: Progressive myopia is related to axial elongation of the eye, not diabetic vascular changes.

stem: Which of the following is true about macular opacity?
options: Only the pupillary margin is visible
Iris and pupillary margins are not visible
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Macular opacity is a central corneal opacity that affects the visual axis.
• In this condition, the central cornea is opaque, while the peripheral cornea remains clear.
• Because of this, only the pupillary margin is visible through the opacity.
• Iris details are partially or completely obscured depending on the size of the opacity.
• Macular opacity can significantly impair vision due to involvement of the central cornea.
• Corneal Opacities: Corneal opacity is formed when Bowman's layer (+/- deeper layer) is damaged. 1. Macular opacity: White in color; Bowman's layer + Half of stromal thickness involved. 2. Nebular opacity: Faint white; Only Bowman's layer is involved; Causes more visual handicap than macular opacity due to the scattering of light. 3. Leucoma: White and opaque BM + half or more than half stromal thickness involved.


Other Options:
Option A: Incorrect the pupil and Iris cannot be fully seen due to central opacity.
Option C: Incorrect - Iris details are obscured by the central opacity.
Option D: Incorrect - some pupillary margin is visible, unlike total opacity.

stem: A patient presents with diminished vision. On torchlight examination, the cornea shows a localized central opacity involving the visual axis. The peripheral cornea is clear. Which of the following clinical descriptions best corresponds to this type of corneal opacity?
options: Only sclera is visible and no iris details can be seen
A faint white opacity allowing partial visualization of the pupillary margin
A dense white opacity completely obscuring the iris and pupil
A peripheral opacity sparing the central visual axis
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• A macular corneal opacity is a moderately dense opacity that typically involves the central cornea and lies over the visual axis.
• Key characteristics include: Central location, Moderate density, Partial visibility of the pupillary margin, Peripheral cornea remains clear.
• Causes significant visual impairment due to involvement of the visual axis.
• Therefore, the description of a faint white opacity with partial visualization of the pupillary margin best fits macular opacity.


Other Options:
Option A: This describes a very dense opacity with no Iris visibility, which is characteristic of leucomatous opacity, not macular opacity.
Option C: Complete obscuration of Iris and pupil suggests a total corneal opacity, which is more severe than macular opacity.
Option D: A peripheral opacity sparing the visual axis describes a nebular or peripheral corneal opacity, which usually causes minimal visual disturbance.

stem: In pterygium, there is:
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Pteryglum is a fibrovascular growth of the conjunctiva that extends onto the cornea, usually from the nasal side.
• It causes astigmatism by flattening the cornea along the axis of the pteryglum, leading to irregular corneal curvature and visual distortion.
• Vision is affected mainly due to Induced astigmatism or encroachment onto the visual axis in advanced cases.
• Early pterygium may be asymptomatic except for cosmetic concerns.
• Surgical removal is indicated If it causes significant astigmatism, visual axis encroachment, or recurrent Irritation.
• Clinical features: 1. Astigmatism with the rule-most common finding 2. Stocker's line deposition of iron on the advancing edge 3. Fuchs Islets-small, discrete, whitish flecks consisting of clusters of pterygial epithelial cells, often present at the advancing edge.


Other Options:
Option A: Pterygium may cause mild irritation but not significant eye pain.
Option B: Macular edema is unrelated to pterygium.
Option C: Purulent discharge is not a feature; pterygium is not an infectious process.

stem: A 6-month-old baby, born preterm at 33 weeks of gestation, is brought by parents with complaints of white pupillary reflex after 2 weeks. CT revealed calcification of the globe in both eyes. What is the probable diagnosis?
subject: Ophthalmology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is a hallmark feature of retinoblastoma in infants.
• Calcification within the globe seen on CT scan is highly suggestive and virtually diagnostic of retinoblastoma.
• Bilateral involvement in a 6-month-old indicates a hereditary form of retinoblastoma.
• Early detection is crucial to preserve life and, if possible, vision, using enucleation or focal therapy depending on the extent.
• Retinoblastoma arises from mutations in the RB1 gene, leading to malignant proliferation of retinal cells.


Other Options:
Option A: ROP (Retinopathy of Prematurity) can cause retinal detachment but does not cause globe calcification.
Option C: Congenital glaucoma presents with buphthalmos, tearing, photophobia, not calcification.
Option D: Congenital cataract causes opacity of the lens, not intraocular calcification.

stem: Which of the following is a cause of bilateral sudden painless loss of vision?
subject: Ophthalmology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Advanced diabetic retinopathy can lead to bilateral sudden painless vision loss due to acute complications.
• Vitreous hemorrhage occurs when fragile neovascular vessels rupture, leading to sudden obscuration of vision.
• Tractional retinal detachment from fibrovascular proliferation can rapidly impair central vision.
• Macular edema in severe cases can cause abrupt central vision loss.
FeaturesCause
Sudden painless loss of visionCentral retinal artery occlusion, Massive vitreous hemorrhage, Retinal detachment involving macula, Ischemic central retinal vein occlusion
Sudden painless onset of diminution of visionCentral serous retinopathy, Methyl alcohol amblyopia, Non-ischemic central retinal vein occlusion
Sudden painful loss of visionAcute congestive glaucoma, Acute Iridocyclitis, Injury to eyeball
Gradual painless diminution of visionCorneal dystrophy, Senile cataract, Primary open angle glaucoma, Optic atrophy, ARMD, Refractive error, Diabetic retinopathy
Gradual painful diminution of visionChronic iridocyclitis, Corneal ulceration



Other Options:
Option A: Acute congestive glaucoma causes sudden painful vision loss, not painless.
Option C: Keratoconus causes gradual visual distortion, not sudden loss.
Option D: Iridocyclitis usually presents with pain, redness, and photophobia, not painless bilateral vision loss.

stem: In FFA, the microaneurysms appear as:
options: Hyperfluorescent branching-like structures in the vessel
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Microaneurysms are focal dilations of retinal capillaries, commonly seen in diabetic retinopathy.
• In Fundus Fluorescein Angiography (FFA), fluorescein dye leaks into these dilations, causing them to appear as bright, hyperfluorescent dots.
• They are usually small, round, and discrete, best visualized in the early phases of FFA.
• Detection of microaneurysms helps in grading diabetic retinopathy and assessing disease progression.
• These hyperfluorescent dots differentiate microaneurysms from hemorrhages, which appear as hypofluorescent areas.


Other Options:
Option A: Hypofluorescent dots correspond to retinal hemorrhages or blocked fluorescence, not microaneurysms.
Option C: Hypofluorescent lines are not characteristic of microaneurysms.
Option D: Hyperfluorescent branching structures represent neovascularization, not microaneurysms.

stem: Normal diameter of artery to vein at the optic disc margin:
subject: Ophthalmology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The central retinal artery (CRA) normally has a diameter approximately two-thirds of the central retinal vein (CRV) at the optic disc margin.
• This 2:3 artery-to-vein ratio is considered physiological and is assessed during fundoscopic examination.
• Decreased ratio (arteriolar narrowing) may indicate hypertension, arteriosclerosis, or other systemic vascular conditions.
• Increased ratio (relative venous narrowing) is less commonly observed but can occur in certain ocular or systemic pathologies.
• Monitoring the AV ratio is useful in early detection of hypertensive retinopathy.


Other Options:
Option A, C, D: These ratios are abnormal and do not represent normal physiological artery-to-vein diameters.

stem: In the case of Best disease, which of the following is the diagnostic investigation?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Electrooculogram (EOG) is the diagnostic investigation of choice in Best disease.
• Best disease (Vitelliform macular dystrophy) is an autosomal dominant macular dystrophy caused by mutations in the BEST1 (VMD2) gene.
• It is characterized by bilateral yellow, yolk-like (vitelliform) lesions at the macula, leading to progressive central vision loss.
• The EOG measures the Arden ratio, which is significantly reduced in Best disease even before clinical signs appear.
• ERG (full-field) is usually normal, as the generalized retinal function is preserved.


Other Options:
Option B: ERG is often normal; not diagnostic.
Option C: Multifocal ERG may detect localized macular dysfunction but is not definitive for Best disease.
Option D: Visual evoked potential evaluates optic nerve function, not macular dystrophies.

stem: A factory worker presented with a chemical injury in both his eyes a few hours back. What is the first immediate management?
options: Check IOP and plan for surgery
subject: Ophthalmology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Immediate copious irrigation of the eyes is the first and most critical step in chemical eye injuries to prevent ongoing damage.
• Irrigation should be done with large volumes of clean water or normal saline, preferably through IV tubing to ensure adequate flow.
• Mechanical removal of residues, such as lime (chuna), should be performed carefully using swabs or sterile instruments.
• Delay in irrigation can lead to corneal epithelial necrosis, stromal damage, and permanent visual loss.
• History, vision assessment, and IOP measurement are important but secondary to immediate irrigation.
• After irrigation, patients should be assessed for pH normalization and ocular surface damage.


Other Options:
Option B: Taking history is important but must not delay irrigation.
Option C: Checking vision is secondary; irrigation takes priority.
Option D: IOP measurement and surgical planning are after initial decontamination.

stem: Which of the following layers of the retina are correctly labeled?
options: 1- Retinal pigment epithelium; 2-Outer nuclear layer; 3- Inner plexiform layer; 4-Ganglion layer
1- Retinal pigment epithelium; 2-Inner nuclear layer; 3-Outer plexiform layer; 4- Ganglion layer
1-Ganglion layer; 2- Inner nuclear layer; 3- Outer plexiform layer; 4-Retinal pigment epithelium
1- Ganglion layer; 2- Outer plexiform layer; 3- Inner nuclear layer; 4- Retinal pigment epithelium
subject: Ophthalmology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The retina consists of multiple layers arranged from the outermost (towards the choroid) to the innermost (towards the vitreous).
• Layer 1 - Retinal pigment epithelium (RPE): Provides metabolic support to photoreceptors and absorbs stray light.
• Layer 2 - Outer nuclear layer: Contains the cell bodies of photoreceptors (rods and cones).
• Layer 3 - Inner plexiform layer: The synaptic layer where bipolar cells connect with ganglion cells.
• Layer 4 - Ganglion cell layer: Contains ganglion cell bodies, whose axons form the optic nerve.
• Correct labeling is essential for understanding retinal physiology, pathologies like retinitis pigmentosa, and macular disorders.
• Layers of Retina (Inner to outer): 1. Internal limiting membrane 2. Nerve fibre layer 3. Ganglion cell layer 4. Inner plexiform layer 5. Inner nuclear layer 6. Outer plexiform layer 7. Outer nuclear layer 8. External limiting membrane 9. Rods and cones 10. Retinal pigment epithelium


Other Options:
Option B: Incorrect - mislabels outer/inner nuclear and plexiform layers.
Option C: Incorrect - reverses orientation; ganglion layer is innermost, not outermost.
Option D: Incorrect - layers are in wrong order and misassigned.
